coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in carbonaceous sustainable nanomaterials for wastewater treatments
coredata.dc:creator: Raza, Ali
Altaf, Shahnila
Ali, Sarfraz
Ikram, Muhammad
Li, Gao
coredata.dc:description: The challengeable situation prevailing throughout the world is still a question mark regarding safe drinking and cleaning for water, and therefore to be resolved on priority basis in concerning research area. In this regard, advancement of environment-friendly supportable nanomaterials possessing unique benefits such as superior efficiency and selectivity, rich earth-abundance, good recyclability, low-cost producing agency, and achievable stability has become a top priority despite several prominent challenges as well as constraints remaining still unresolved. Carbonaceous nanomaterials especially like activated carbon, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, single-walled carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots have been developed and their application as adsorbents toward wastewater treatment anchored with its purification; graphene (GR) and graphene oxide (GO) derivatives and other carbon-derivative nanomaterials like graphitic carbon nitrides as well as carbon sponges/aerogels have imparted significant contribution for water associated with wastewater treatment and remediation (adsorption, membrane technologies, advanced oxidation processes), especially on the industrial level along with pharmaceutical-laden wastages. This review envelopes developed technologies, methodologies, and surface modifications regarding carbonaceous nanomaterials for removal of pollutants along with ionic metals in an aqueous medium, and proving novelty regarding nanoadsorbents for a variety of issues such as wastewater, drinking water, and groundwater remediation. Additionally, heterojunctions strategies are also addressed with current alarming trends and resolvable challenges pertaining to sustainable carbon and its derivatives unified with substantial appliances while handling and purifying wastewater are suggestively highlighted.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in carbonaceous sustainable nanomaterials for wastewater treatments

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The challengeable situation prevailing throughout the world is still a question mark regarding safe drinking and cleaning for water, and therefore to be resolved on priority basis in concerning research area. In this regard, advancement of environment-friendly supportable nanomaterials possessing unique benefits such as superior efficiency and selectivity, rich earth-abundance, good recyclability, low-cost producing agency, and achievable stability has become a top priority despite several prominent challenges as well as constraints remaining still unresolved. Carbonaceous nanomaterials especially like activated carbon, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, single-walled carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots have been developed and their application as adsorbents toward wastewater treatment anchored with its purification; graphene (GR) and graphene oxide (GO) derivatives and other carbon-derivative nanomaterials like graphitic carbon nitrides as well as carbon sponges/aerogels have imparted significant contribution for water associated with wastewater treatment and remediation (adsorption, membrane technologies, advanced oxidation processes), especially on the industrial level along with pharmaceutical-laden wastages. This review envelopes developed technologies, methodologies, and surface modifications regarding carbonaceous nanomaterials for removal of pollutants along with ionic metals in an aqueous medium, and proving novelty regarding nanoadsorbents for a variety of issues such as wastewater, drinking water, and groundwater remediation. Additionally, heterojunctions strategies are also addressed with current alarming trends and resolvable challenges pertaining to sustainable carbon and its derivatives unified with substantial appliances while handling and purifying wastewater are suggestively highlighted.

ce:section.ce:para: The 21st century has been designated as the age of environmental study [1]. Through increasing global community, an expansion of industrial and agricultural activities, soils pollution, aquatic, air, and biodiversity, and, ultimately, global climate modification, environmental issues have risen to the top of science and political priority lists [2–5]. Presently, there exists a worldwide determination to recognize the impact of human actions on the environment with respect to the development of new technologies to diminish the environmental and health implications. Aside from diverse approaches to reporting on the above urgent environmental issues, recent signs of advancement in nanotechnology have spurred an elevated interest in using nanomaterials' exceptional properties for use in environmentally sustainable applications. In this regard, various nanomaterials hold distinctive properties, due to their association of nanoscale dimensions can be engaged to expand the enactment of prevailing processes or to design novel approaches.

Nanomaterials have originated numerous applications towards energy production, contaminant sensing, and water treatment, thus growing literature calls how innovative nanomaterials can be utilized to state main environmental challenges [6–12]. Currently, carbon nanoparticles are reflected as resourceful materials that may be engaged towards enhancement in polluted water treatment. Globally, broad research was carried out that stemmed from the innovation of novel carbon-based substances that can be effectively employed in eco-friendly approaches, for wastewater management [13–17]. The latest literature reports the core objectives such as pathogenic microorganisms (micropollutants) that can be extracted from wastewater with the aid of carbon nanoparticles headed for numerous technological methods [17–25]. Their unique physiochemical properties regulate a range of practical applications.

Generally, GR material, their derivatives GO; single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) are the most frequently inspected carbon-based nanomaterials. These discussed materials can be utilized as complex hybrid materials, or commonly in their pure forms [16,26–33]. Water supply methods and wastewater treatment are the next generation approaches that depend upon its economic feasibility, and ecological approachability [13,34]. These nanomaterials own a high surface area to volume ratio that enables them to boost reactivity with pathogens or environmental pollutants such as viruses, fungi, or bacteria [35–38]. GR derivatives correspond to the significant branch of carbon derivative that is designated as GO. A functional group comprising oxygen is bound to the GR layer forming stable suspensions in aqueous media because its composition requires a high concentration attributing to oxygen functional groups namely, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy residing in carbon lattice [2,39–44]. This property permits GO to be utilized in practical applications such as in water treatment. Furthermore, GO is characterized through supplementary unique features that comprise tunable pore size, abundant and selective sites for adsorption, small intra-particle diffusion distance, superior charge carriers mobility, high specific surface area, and exceptional charge conductivity. High adsorption capacity and mechanical strength permits GO to produce hybrid nanocomposites with many materials. Significantly GO can be characterized through low production cost and simple manufacturing [3,45–49].

This Review article has been designed as follows: firstly in sections, we briefly discuss the carbon-based nanomaterials with particular characteristics for photocatalysis and other water treatments schemes. Afterward, section 2 moves towards various carbon-based nanomaterials including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, GR, graphitic carbon nitride, and carbon sponge/aerogel with fundamentals and advances in water research. The GR section will cover the existing advanced strategies for photocatalysis including heterojunction photocatalysts, Schottky junctions, Z-scheme heterojunctions, and multicomponent heterojunctions discussion on these parameters will provide a pathway for better and improvement for water purification schemes. Finally, sections 3 and 4 provide several key current difficulties and potential solutions, as well as the authors' recommendations are presented (Fig. 1 ).

Adsorption has been suggested as the most useful technique to remove contamination from the liquids owing to its favorable characteristics e.g. low cost, easy to operate, and no by-products. However, traditional adsorbent efficiency is constantly restricted and hence inhibits the process of removing contaminants. Nanostructured compounds contain broad specific range and associated adsorption sites with tuned surface qualities in comparison with standard adsorbents offer them some advantages, including high performances and/or quick adsorption speeds covering a wide range of pH [20,50,51].

Porous carbon materials have been extensively employed in water treatment for a long time. However, their adsorption capacity was already challenged for a great many years and their limited regeneration ability was been challenged. Surface structure, surface chemical characteristics, and electrochemical qualities are all factors that influence adsorption and regeneration ability. Some modified approaches, including as oxidation, reduction, and metal doping, are being explored to address these issues [52,53]. Among these approaches, oxidation or reduction alters the characteristics of porous carbon by altering the quantity of acidic or basic groups on the surface, causing the adsorbents to adsorb the base or acid dyes present in the effluent. Regardless, both treatments cause severe surface and pore harm to carbon [54]. Metal doping is the fine dispersion of metal particles on porous carbon surfaces and pore channels [55,56]. Adding metal to porous carbon helps to preserve the overall integrity of the porous carbon surface and pore structure while also changing the surface characteristics of porous carbon, which improves its capacity to absorb contaminants. Furthermore, the newly produced active metal sites may be able to coordinate with the carbon matrix in order to increase its selectivity, which is responsible for the outstanding specific interaction between metal and nonmetallic components [52,57]. The magnetic property of the metal itself may also play a role in the increase of the adsorbent's regeneration ability. Consequently, metal-doped porous carbon compounds are becoming increasingly popular among researchers. Metal ions are categorized into three categories: hard, soft, and boundary acid. Hard acid is the most reactive of the three. In response to the doping of metal ions on the porous carbon surface, the acid content of the surface rose dramatically [58,59]. Afterwards, it reacts with the base groups on the adsorbates surface, resulting in an enhancement in the removal ability. Additionally, it was discovered that metal doping may alter the surface electronegativity of porous carbon material, so increasing the electrostatic attraction between the porous carbon material and the organic dye with the opposite charge, thereby improving the adsorption performance. As a result, it may be that altering the surface characteristics of carbon materials by doping metal might result in improved adsorption performance. However, associated with metal characteristics of most adsorbents, they may be readily removed from the solution once the adsorption process has taken place.

Numerous carbonaceous compounds, including various dyes [38,43,60–62], a variety of antibiotics [63], polycyclic aromatic rings hydrocarbons [64], phenol group compounds [65], harmful pesticides [66] along with others have shown exceptional adsorption efficiency in GR-based nanostructured materials. The malathion, endosulfan, and chlorpyrifos concentrations over reduced graphene oxide (rGO) adsorption can reach up to 800, 1100, and 1200 mg grGO -1, respectively, in liquid phases [66]. Many carbonaceous nanomaterials, containing carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as well as fullerenes, and nanocomposites, have been widely analyzed and tested for their ability to remove organic compounds from wastewater. Their potential adsorption capability is primarily attributed to their high specific surface area, random pores distribution, possible surface properties, enabling them vigorous reactive organic compounds through hydrophobic effect, hydrogen bonding along with energy decomposition analysis (EDA) interactions. Furthermore, studies have shown that normal environmental conditions and surface characteristics of adsorbents may influence adsorptive capability, and hence a slight difference between adsorption efficiency. The aggregation of CNTs in the liquid phase creates several interstitial spaces associated with grooves, providing efficient energy adsorption areas, thereby serving to dissolve organic compounds. GO containing various oxygenic groups (such as, COOH as well as –OH) on the edges may degrade hydrophobic influence through nonpolar organic compounds, thereby preventing adsorption. Large-sized compounds, such as algal toxins, are inaccessible to nanoporous activated carbon due to their micro-porous composition, although it may be useful for comparably small molecules [20,61].

A group of researchers has conducted extensive literature work on the heavy metal adsorption properties owing to GO as well as oxidizing CNTs, offering collective incorporation of GO with CNTs exhibited higher adsorption efficiency associated with rapid adsorption rate towards metal remediation [67,68]. The ability of organic materials to adsorb a variety of heavy metal ions, such as Cd2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+ have been analyzed [69]. Active participation owing to carbonate compounds to adsorb heavy metal ions has been extensively examined identified with metals such as Cd2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ [70]. Chemical bonding caused by electrostatic attraction will dominate adsorption technique, containing GO functional groups along with oxidized CNTs that provide majority of adsorption areas existing in heavy metal ions. The highly accessible adsorption edges and the limited intra-particle diffusion gap may be attributed to fast adsorption kinetics (Fig. 2b-e). Also, the removal of heavy metals was provided much consideration by different nanostructured materials and nanocomposites.

The photocatalytic process is a progressive oxidation method to remove pathogens and trace out contaminants present in wastewater that is regarded as favorable prior treatment technique for toxic molecules as well as non-biodegradable containments to upgrade biodegradability [30,71,72]. At the moment, several studies focused on photocatalysis enhancement through carbonaceous materials are being vigorously investigated. Carbonaceous nanomaterials usage such as CNTs, rGO, and fullerenes chemically interacted with catalysts is a hot topic (TiO2 and ZnO, etc.). By introducing TiO2 on separated GR nanosheets, they may occupy synergetic properties derived by fabrication methods, thus increasing photocatalysis efficiency for desired pollutants. Yang et al. [73] developed TiO2/GR composite assembly along with CNTs and fullerenes nanomaterials photocatalysts while converting benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde, by claiming the associated nanocomposites could expand adsorption sites towards visible light response. Various hybrid photocatalysts were used for organic pollutants decontamination in addition to TiO2/carbonaceous nanomaterials such as ZnS/RGO photocatalysts [74] as well as ZnO-GR. In reality, GR and CNTs could have wide surface area advantages and/or particle restriction for photocatalysts, similar to TiO2. The possessive carbonaceous nanocomposites may upgrade photocatalysis performance in many ways, such as by providing superior quality adsorption edge sites, by introducing photo electrons movements, and interfering with electron-hole pair recombination; and also bandgap tuning or photosensitization respectively [75].

Disinfection is an efficient method to eliminate organic and biological contaminants including pathogens from wastewater. Typical methods such as chlorination and ozonation would generate unsafe disinfection by-products in the decontamination procedure and endanger human health. Nanotechnology advancements shed a youthful insight for reducing the weaknesses of these conventional approaches. Carbonaceous nanocomposites have considerable antimicrobial performance caused by less oxidation potential, maybe greatly inactivated microbes irradiated visible light and/or direct interaction, possessing a lower ability to produce disinfection by-products. For the last few years, GR-based nanomaterials have been extensively examined as disinfectants [76,77]. Magnetic GR produced by Ganesh et al. [78] demonstrated disinfected performance (42 ug L-1) for E.coli with 98% bacteria destroying towards disinfection applications for minimal toxicity for zebrafish (Fig. 2a).

However, magnetic rGO functionalized with glutaraldehyde nanomaterials harvested and presented by Wu et al. [79] has demonstrated a strong disinfection propensity of up to 99 percent toward S. aureus (gram-positive) and E. coli bacteria towards infrared irradiated laser light. The antimicrobial activity, high surface area, thereby offering substantial conductivity owing to GR derivatives nanocomposites make possible use. However, the anti-microorganisms mechanism owing to carbonaceous nanocomposites was defined by some important aspects. First is damaging cell membrane integrity through direct contact whereas second is disrupting real microbial progress through oxidative stress. Furthermore, additional studies into optimizing disinfection performance and the environmental impact on carbonaceous nanomaterials must be prioritized.

In many areas of water purification, membrane methodology was considered an efficient and economical technique, and its efficiency is caused heavily by selectivity as well as permeability owing to membrane materials. Further, large energy consumption depending upon membrane fouling has hampered processes' widespread utility. Significant research has indicated that incorporating carbonaceous nanocomposites into membranes can improve selectivity as well as permeability, and/or membrane fouling hindrance [80,81]. CNTs may be associated with some polymers or nanocomposites to create multi-aspect membranes that can be used successfully in membrane technology, as seen in Fig. 3 . Among those who have contributed to this work are Song et al. [82]. CNTs can be injected into double-layer thin-film membranes to characterize and/or develop morphology, composition, saturation efficiency, and antifouling capability owing to integrated membranes. Thin-film nanocomposites-based membranes were thought to have possible water fluxing and extraordinary antifouling capacity due to the incorporation of CNTs. According to Ho et al. [83], the integrated GO and oxidized CNTs had a possible impact on the characterization of membranes containing surface charge porous area, mechanical strength, hardness, pore size and shape, and other indicators. As a result, complex matrix membranes can improve membranes with respect to hydrophilicity as well as antifouling properties, through enhancing rejection rates relevant to organic and/or inorganic contaminants. Another kind of carbonaceous filter is free-standing carbonaceous nanofiber membranes that have the valuable surface area along with a greater degree of uniformity and a wide number of active sites. Beyond traditional adsorption techniques, they associate attractive efficiency owing to carbonaceous nanofiber and favorable advantage attributed with nanofiber membrane blocking, combined with high permeability as well as antifouling capability. Carbonaceous nanofiber membranes can rapidly strip dyes such as methylene blue (MB) with high density (1580 L m-2 h-1), regarding greater as compared with the most industrial ultrafiltration membranes possessing comparable properties [84]. In addition, other catalysts may also be integrated simultaneously with various mechanisms in carbonaceous membranes for pollutant removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various carbonaceous nanomaterials for wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The distinctive characteristics of hybrid-carbon sp2 bonds will combine carbon nanomaterials with extraordinary nanoscale physicochemical properties; usually, they exhibit excellent electric, mechanical and chemical characteristics. These nanomaterials are categorized into many forms based on their composition, electrical, chemical properties (Fig. 4 ). In different applications worldwide they have attracted attention for environmental removal, pollutants remediation, drug delivery, photocatalysis, catalysis, and supercapacitors, respectively. In the coming future, the position of carbonated nanocomposites may be increasingly promising by the booming development of nanotechnologies.

1D structure has substantial specific surface area and guided electron movement. These structures have a bigger surface area than bulk, increasing surface reaction sites and decreasing electron/hole recombination rate. Their one shape also provides for high interfacial charge carrier transfer rates because to the reduced diffusion distance. Thus, these factors benefit photocatalytic processes.

They have enormous specific surface areas and plenty of low-coordinated surface atoms. The conduction band minimum is shifted higher by surface lattice distortion (oxygen vacancies and low-coordinated surface atoms). With its narrow bandgap and high surface reactive sites content, the structure is effective in using solar light, causing strong photocatalytic activity (hydrogen production pollutant degradation) and good stability during recycling in simulated solar radiation.

3D structures are usually made up of 0D, 1D, and 2D morphologies, so they may combine their benefits. Also, 3D photocatalysts outperform 2D photocatalysts. Generally, an ordered morphology promotes charge carrier movement and hence photocatalytic activity. Table 1 explores recent investigations on carbon nanomaterials for wastewater treatments.

The surface chemistry and reaction process of carbon-based properties were studied extensively to increase the absorption of certain pollutants by these adsorbents [130–133]. It is commonly established that functional groups may directly attach to fused aromatic rings in hydrocarbons. So their chemistry should be similar to aromatic hydrocarbons. Similar chemical processes involving aromatic rings and functional groups can be undertaken, allowing thermal or chemical design of adsorbents with desired properties. For instance, utilizing oxidation processes, scientists created weakly acidic surface functions on activated carbons [134–136]. In contrast to pure carbon, these carbons were used as metal adsorbents because of their extraordinary affinity for metals. A great deal of research has also been done on heteroatom doping, particularly in particular nitrogen doping. These carbons have demonstrated increased catalytic activity throughout the oxidation process [137,138]. The specific adsorption processes for organic and inorganic solutes are still unknown even though activated carbon is a growing subject of study in wastewater treatment. For the adsorption of organic molecules, a recently hypothesized process involving dispersion interaction has been presented [139,140]. This method implies that aromatic species and basal planes in carbon interact with π electrons. Some experimental results indicate the electron donor-acceptor mechanism drives organic adsorption, citing complex formation between adsorbed species and the sorbent's surface carbonyl group. This study summarizes recent achievements in synthesis of surface-modified activated carbons and diverse modification approaches and their impact on energy storage, adsorption of contaminants from gaseous and liquid phases. Activation of carbon-based materials produces a porous structure accompanied by surface modification.

In order to acidify the surface of activated carbon, the surface is typically oxidized, which increases the acidic character and hydrophilic nature of the carbon surface by decreasing the mineral content of the carbon surface. This is accomplished primarily through the use of HNO3, H2SO4, and H3PO4 [141,142]. Oxidation causes the formation of carboxylic, lactone, and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon, which are oxygen-enriched functionalities. Extraction of oxygen-derived acidic groups by oxidation with gases and by aqueous oxidants are the two most often employed activation processes for creating oxygen-derived acidic groups. In gas-phase oxidation, oxidants such as carbon dioxide, oxygen, steam, and air are utilized. In contrast to the creation of strong acidic functionalities (e.g., carboxylic groups) during oxidation at low temperatures, the creation of weak acidic functionalities (e.g., hydroxyl groups) occurs during oxidation at raised temperatures (e.g., phenolic). Because liquid phase oxidation can result in a greater oxygen concentration at surfaces as compared to gas-phase oxidation, it consumes less energy than the latter. Moreover, gas-phase oxidation can increase the amount of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups present on the surface of activated carbon, whereas liquid phase oxidation predominantly increases the amount of carboxylic and phenolic hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the activated carbon. Applicable acidic groups (oxygen-containing functional groups as proton donors) on carbon surfaces can be used to adsorb heavy metals from aqueous solutions by forming metal complexes with negatively charged acidic groups. Multiple experimental efforts have been directed at improving the removal of metal ions and organic molecules from aqueous solutions by altering the carbon surface by inserting acidic groups. Carbonyl, lactone, hydroxyl, and carboxylic anhydride are just a few of the acidic surface functional moieties that may be found [130]. These functional groups are predisposed to form metal complexes with alkaline-earth metals and chelate heavy metal ions, respectively. Complex formation with main group metals is represented as equation 1:
(1)

n +

↔

nH +

A positive charge on the surface of activated carbon from a base treatment promotes the adsorption of negatively charged molecules. The easiest technique to make porous carbons with fundamental surface features is to heat them with hydrogen or ammonia. The surface of activated carbons treated with ammonia at high temperatures (400–900°C) forms basic nitrogen groups. It can be doped by reacting with nitrogen precursors (such as ammonia, nitric acid, and different amines) or activating in a nitrogen-rich environment [143]. Nitrogen can exist in the form of amide, imide, lactame, pyrrolic, and pyridinic groups. The main purpose of ammonia treatment is to increase the surface basicity of activated carbon. Aside from adding basic nitrogen atoms at high temperatures, excluding oxygen-based groups increases the basicity of activated carbons. Adsorbents and acidic species interact via dipole-dipole interactions, hydrogen bonding, and covalent bonding when nitrogen functional groups are present. Preparation of the adsorbent for ammonia treatment by Przepiorski [144]. The absorption capacity for phenol was enhanced by up to 29% at 700°C. In another work, Chen et al. [145] examined how heat treatment with ammonia modified activated carbon surfaces, increasing perchlorate adsorption capacity in aqueous circumstances without altering the porous structure. They found that samples processed between 650 and 700 oC had the best outcomes, with a four-fold increase in perchlorate adsorption. The great performance of nitrogen-doped activated carbons generated at high temperatures (600–900oC) was demonstrated by Stöhr et al. [146]. Free radicals (e.g., NH2, NH, hydrogen, and nitrogen) develop when carbon-based materials are heated in an ammonia-rich environment. To create nitrogen-based functional groups [147], free radicals attack the carbon surface. Amination of carboxylic acid groups on the carbon surface can yield ammonium salts that dehydrate to amides and nitriles (equation 2):
(2)
− COO −
NH 4 +
→ − CO −
NH 2
→ C ≡ N

Substitution of OH groups with ammonia can result in the formation of amines (equation 3):
(3)

Since their discovery in 1985, fullerenes have been considered third allotropic form of carbon material type. They are encircled by cage-like nature structures composed of twelve pentagon rings and a general number referred to as six-member rings, which are commonly observed in the form of six-member rings connected by carbon atom sites. As an example, buckyball (C60) contains 32 sides, out of which 12 pentagons whereas 20 hexagons, and total carbon atoms are chained in a truncated icosahedron with 60 vertices is suggested greatly symmetric [148,149]. The development of fullerenes and their derivatives is displayed in Fig. 5a. Structures containing decreased hexagons have a higher sp3 bonding behavior associated with large strain energies, incorporated with a greater supply of reactive sites identified with carbon. Furthermore, fullerenes show such chemical properties that are numerous and diverse, allowing for differences in manipulating and associated environmental applications. To achieve goal of functional applications, various structural measuring strategies such as covalent bonding, endohedral, and/or super-molecular-transformations were employed. Moreover, a variety of approaches including OH as well as COOH groups will improve dispersion mechanism particularly towards pristine fullerenes associated with polymers, thereby changing the mechanical and electrical properties [20]. C60 hydrophilic functionalized fullerenes can be utilized to kill the microorganism’s pathogens in water through utilizing a photocatalytic activity, mechanism as shown in Fig. 5b-e. Due to the low carbon and hydrogen bond energies of fullerene molecules, which enable them to be quickly changed into C-C bonds to C-H, fullerenes are also discovered to be model cleaning agents for hydrogen storage. Fullerenes may hold up to 6.1 % hydrogen has their chemistry and cage-like molecular shape, and their structure is easily reversed because of their greater C-C bond energies [41].

Fullerene-based nanocomposites have a lot of biomedical applications and various researches showed their biocompatibility using living species. It can also be utilized as a biosensor and bioreceptor [151–153]. The fullerene is a toxic-free substance that may be misused for filtration, adsorbents as a solid membrane for environmental and wastewater treatment applications.

In 1999, CNTs were found by Lijima and might be SWCNTs and MWCNTs for instance layered rolled-up GR and multilayered rolled-up GR respectively [154–156]. CNTs have sparked a lot of interest in the field of nanotechnology after they were discovered because of their distinctive physicochemical nature. These carbon allotropes nanorods have extensive applications in several fields such as field emission, semiconductor, energy-storing systems, water, and air filtration and medicine, etc. Their diameters range from one to several nanometers, high surface area (150-1500 m2 g-1), and the presence of mesopores, render them efficient substances to remove heavy metal ions through adsorption and advanced oxidation processes. Furthermore, other organic materials can be used to functionalize CNTs, making them ideal for adsorbate selection and enhancing their adsorption capacity. The sorption of heavy metal ions across carbon nanotubes is affected by surface morphology, ion exchange strategies, and electrochemical ability [31,41,157,158].

Adsorption technique is relay on the physicochemical properties of the adsorbent include unique surface area, hydrophobicity, surface functional groups, and chemical bonds such as hydrogen bonds, electrostatic, π–π bond [28,159]. Fig. 6a shows a schematic display of an adsorption phenomenon of a vast number of adsorbents. The surface wettability of CNTs is altered by functional groups, resulting in hydrophilicity and improved adsorption of polar compounds with low molecular weight. Researchers investigated the adsorptive acetaminophen (AAP) elimination of MWCNTs modified chemically by NaOH/HNO3 or H2SO4, ozone, and chitosan (Fig. 6b, c) [160]. The addition of carboxylic groups, -OH, and –COO groups to the surface of MWCNTs alters the size and shape of pores, and consequently acetaminophen adsorption increases without allowing the crystal structure to rupture. The adsorption abilities of adapted MWCNTs were in order of (250 mgAAP g-1) for ozone-treated MWCNTs > chitosan-coated MWCNTs (205 mgAAP g-1) > acidic MWCNTs (160 mgAAP g-1) > NaOH treated (130 mgAAP g-1) > (90 mgAAP g-1) for MWCNTs. Similarly, MWCNTs that had been modified or oxidized with hypochlorite were tested for ciprofloxacin elimination. According to scientists, the rate of increase in adsorption potential slowed during the π–π electron donor-acceptor interaction (see Fig. 7 ) [161]. Therefore, the evolution of hydrophilicity & dispersibility showed a significant role for ciprofloxacin adsorption into oxidized MWCNTs. Several studies have demonstrated CNTs adsorption interaction and an organic compound.

Adsorption phenomena can be dominated by EDA interactions, such as π-π and n-π EDA interactions. During the adsorption process, π-electron depleted and π–electron-rich rings or CNTs aromatic zones, as well as goal adsorbents, are the main causes of π–π EDA-interactions. CNTs can act as π-donors and π-acceptors. Aromatic structures with high electron density can produce larger quadrupoles, resulting in rich electron adsorbates as a π-donor and CNTs surface as a π-acceptor [165]. Conversely, lowering aromatic structure electron density can oppose the quadrupoles moment and form adsorbate electron-deficient (π-acceptor). But CNTs, for example, will behave like electron-rich (π-donors) GR sheets with a polarized end and adsorbate donor electron may bind to a high-electric density defective area [166,167]. A different relationship is likely when electron unshared pairs of amino or hydroxyl groups behave like a donor species and associate with electron-deficient edges of CNTs that act as electron acceptors [168]. Aromatic compounds enclosing hydroxyl group and CNTs can demonstrate improved adsorption because of heavy EDA interaction between electrons donated hydroxyl group and CNTs π electron-deficient areas. Ionized amino and hydroxyl groups strengthen n-π electron EDA interaction at high pH due to their strong electron-donating groups (Fig. 6d) [164].

Hydrogen bonding is aided by carbon molecules or CNTs with functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2. In this case, the adsorbates benzene rings serve as hydrogen-bonding acceptors whereas these functional moieties can act as hydrogen-bonding donors and form hydrogen bonds with CNTs graphite surfaces. The functional groups on the surface of CNTs that serve as hydrogen-bonding donors, such as –COOH and –OH, could also form bonds with organic elements. Instead of adsorbate chemicals, strong H-bonding interactions between CNT functional groups and water can cause competitive sorption between CNTs and organic compounds. As a result, minor hydrogen bonding effects on solute adsorption without a hydrogen-bonding tendency will occur [164,168]. Lewis acid-base concept has been elucidated as an additional touch for target molecule adsorption using amino groups as a significant method described before can be deduced as a bonding contact between adsorbates and adsorbents. The electronic features of adsorbate materials may have a significant impact on the adsorption process [169].

Numerous semiconducting materials are utilized such as Fe3O4, ZnO, and TiO2, as these materials have low quantum efficiency, and their ultraviolet response is not high [170–172]. Owing to their excellent quantum performance, nanoscale, greater chemical stability, empty tube structure, and extended light adsorption zone related to their large specific surface area, carbon nanotubes are intriguing catalytic materials [173]. Gao et al. [174] successfully used ultrathin photocatalyst-based SWCNTs-TiO2 for oil-water purification. Park et al. [175] successfully removed MB from water by incorporating titania on SWCNTs aerogel. Furthermore, Xu et al. [176] successfully removed pyridine from water using photocatalysts made from MWCNTs hydroxyl and PbO3 nanostructured anodes (Fig. 8a, b) and used it successfully for pyridine removal from water. Later on, Lee et al. [177] generated MWCNTs-TiO2 for photodegradation of MB as shown in Fig. 8c, d.

In the near past, Chen et al. [178] successfully synthesized CNT/WSe2 composites via a one-pot solvothermal technique by reacting WSe2 nanosheets with highly conductive CNTs system. And the harvested product CNT/WSe2 was first time tested for photocatalytic potential against methylene orange (MO) degradation (Fig. 9c). The original flowerlike WSe2 tends to agglomerate and have low conductivity, but CNTs based WSe2 composite is found to enhance the charge and hole separation and reduce the electron-hole recombination, consequently, increases the availability of OH radical and the number of holes that act as active sites for photocatalytic reaction. CNT mounted WSe2 composites are found to show superb photocatalytic properties due to the presence of CNTs which not only provide a very conducive WSe2 layer but also stop the accumulation of WSe2 (Fig. 9a, b).

A possible degradation pathway of sulfamethazine (SMZ) by the photocatalytic treatment has been suggested based on the monitoring of the transformation products (Fig. 10b). In 2020, Samy et al. [179] successfully synthesized CNTs/LaVO4 nanoparticles in various ratios and then their potential efficacy being a photocatalyst was probed by degradation of SMZ as shown in Fig. 10a. Effective interaction between CNTs and LaVO4 moieties was confirmed through characterization via different analytical techniques and it was observed that 0.3% CNTs/LaVO4 are more efficient photocatalyst as compared to lower concentration (0.1%) of CNTs/LaVO4. In case of response surface methodology, the ideal reaction conditions include 3.0 pH, 0.25 g L-1 of catalyst, and 153 W m-2 of light intensity. However, in suspension mode with an initial concentration of 30 mg L-1 of SMZ and reaction conditions of pH = 7, light intensity of 146 W m-2 for 150 min, the degradation efficiency of 0.1 g L-1 of 0.3% CNTs/LaVO4 was found lower than detection limit. Moreover, the reusability of CNTs/LaVO4 in dip coating mode was investigated, while CNTs/LaVO4 was immobilized on aluminum plates using polysiloxane. Degradation efficiencies were rechecked and reaction was repeated 5-times by using 0.3% CNTs/LaVO4 immobilized catalyst at pH of 7, with 146 W m-2 light intensity for 180 min (Fig. 10c, d). The use of scavengers in photocatalytic degradation process exposed that holes were responsible for catalytic activity. Investigation of the formed products suggested the possible degradation mechanism of photocatalytic treatment for SMZ.

Application of SWCNTs and MWCNTs in filtration membrane exhibited significant outcomes using high potentially personal care products (PPCPs) approximately (10-95%) and endocrine compounds (EDCs) ranging (60.4-95.2%) removals, thus describing CNT’s efficiency for wastewater management [180,181]. The percentages of upper-bound removal are operated experimentally in the lack of natural organic matter (NOM) and CNTs, while the percentages of lower-bound removal are owing to the presence of NOM or NOM/CNTs during filtration experiments [182]. The NOM presence badly affects PPCPs and EDCs removal. For instance, researches have shown that Suwannee River fulvic (SRFA), a normalized NOM, badly influences the removal rate of triclosan. Triclosan removal rates in the absence of SRFA using MWCNTs were > 90%, and triclosan removal rate decreased upto 63% in the presence of SRFA [180]. Same observations were recorded in the presence of NOM for bisphenol AF (BPA) and 17β-estradiol (E2) removal with SWCNTs, and removal rates were found to be 60.4% and 79.7% but in the lack of NOM, results were noted to be 78.4% and 94.9%, which elucidates the bad effects of NOM’s on removal proficiency owing to its competition using target adsorbate [28,180,183]. In addition, NOM creates a problem because of its function as carcinogenic disinfection (by-products) and pollutes the membrane during wastewater treatment [184,185]. However, NOM removal is beneficial for human fitness and wastewater treatment. In order to understand starring role of NOM in removing the PPCPs and EDCs, a study of basic phenomena between CNTs and normalized NOM SFRA is mandatory. Researches elucidated that initially, SRFA composed of aromatic structure can intermingle with CNTs through π–π electron donor/acceptor interaction. It is interesting to know that PPCPs and EDCs can also interact with CNTs using a similar approach, and the competition of NOM and PPCPs adsorption will decrease the PPCPs elimination. The similar interaction of SRFA-CNTs and PPCPs-CNTs indicates that SRFA presence will reduce the PPCPs’ adsorption. In addition, hydrophobic interaction or hydrogen bonding also promotes the adsorption of SRFA onto CNTs [180]. Contrary to the above discussion, some investigators directed that NOM might help in removal of BPA and E2 through NOM-partitioning, however, opposite results are obtained from the present study [185,186]. NOM may have competed with BPA and E2 for CNTs adsorption suggesting a lower removal proficiency of these compounds. Competition among NOM and target adsorbents for free interstitial spaces can chunk SWNTs pores. Membranes carrying CNTs used for filtration are found very favorable for their complete application in water treatment procedures.

CNTs are extensively used in various sections such as drug delivery, biosensors, diagnosis, semiconductors, electronic and environmental issues [190]. Many pieces of research exhibited CNTs biocompatibility as it was mostly exploited in biomedical applications [191,192]. Carbonaceous materials such as CNTs are safely employed for environmental issues and specifically for water treatment. GR-based materials for environmental crisis and adsorption of gaseous pollutants, CO2 is an environmental problem since it contributes to global warming [193]. Nanomaterials were also found to be significant substance relative to conventional substances with respect to proficiency and cost [194]. GR-based substances have been employed to adsorb gas pollutants [195]. Gosh et al. [195] exhibited constructive application of GR-based substances for tapping of CO2 and H2; monolayer GR was recorded to trap CO2 (37.93%). GR has been selectively described to adsorb CO2 compare to CH4 and N2) gases. Selective GO for CO2 might be ascribed to greater dipole moment of CO2 which may easily contact with polar oxygenated CO2 functional groups [196]. Many studies have been stated for GR chemistry tuning and enhanced selectivity of favorable gaseous contaminant [196,197]. GR based nanomaterials are excellent for organic dyes removal from water owing to its nanosized large surface area, tendency to interact with π-π stacking, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding [198]. GO was also being used to remove cationic dyes like MB, crystal-violet (CV), and rhodamine B (RhB) from water. It is obvious that there is a directly proportional relationship between primary dye concentration and adsorption, using adsorption abilities of 199.2, 195.4, and 154.8 mgg-1 for MB, CV, and RhB respectively [199]. Though pollutants might be removed from water through adsorption technique this approach is incapable to degrade pollutants and disposal step is mandatory [200]. Photocatalytic activity is the most useful method for wastewater treatment to completely degrade and mineralization of biological/organic pollutants [201]. Owing to high surface area, nanosize, and extra electronic properties GR-based photocatalysts are proved better as compared to traditionally used materials [201,202]. Rommozzi et al. [203] fabricated rGO through a greener reduction scheme with glucose (C6H12O6) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) positively designed a photocatalyst visible by fabricated with TiO2. The engineered rGO-TiO2 photocatalyst was effectively probed for refractory various dyes [204].

This procedure has been regarded as a revolutionary form of water desalination treatment at MIT (USA), where it could be used as sustainable reverse osmosis (RO) membrane. Water permeability is poor in traditional RO membranes, necessitating high operating power for the desalination strategy [22,205–208]. This new membrane includes the modification of GR via generating nanopores above the surface of GR. The use of well-structured pore channels will simplify the flow of water and thus achieve a quick flow using this process. Furthermore, not only salt can be removed, but some molecules, based on their molecular size, can also be filtered by adjusting the size of the pores. To remove ions and other solute compounds, the openings employ physical concepts like hydrophobicity and charge transformation [107,208–214]. The entire desalination process is depicted in Fig. 13 ; GR has several pros over other traditional membranes used in water desalination research. Since GR is just one atom thick, its hardness can be overlooked, and this is its most important advantage. GR, on the other hand, has high mechanical strength and low-pressure requirements, allowing for easier water transport [206,209,215]. As a result, the issue of how to perforate something harder than diamond arises. The nanopores that are needed for water desalination on GR can be created using two methods. Furthermore, since the scale of the pores must be precise, the perforating route must be carried out with special care and precision. Desalination would not be possible due to the small pore sizes that prevent water molecules from passing through [209].

Nowadays, scientists have established a proficient approach for the production of essential pores above GR sheets. In this step, with the help of gallium ion on GR sheet is thoroughly examined, whilst an unfocused laser is fired at the GR sheet, allowing gallium ions to scatter throughout the GR sheet, causing disorders in the material's lattice and hitting off carbon atoms. As a consequence, the etchable flaws are formed, and GR is used in an acidic potassium permanganate solution that is commonly used to open CNTs. Pore size changes with the time duration of immersing GR sheet in an oxidizing solution. More time to immersed GR in the solution caused to enlarge size of the pores. The outcomes of using such a technique were monolayer GR that confined five trillion 0.4 nm holes per cm [206]. For desalination initiatives, the full pore diameter must be ~ 7 Å through experimental research [205,209]. More research on the declared process for use in large GR filters should also be conducted. After pores were made, some hydrophilic bonds were created to increase water flow through the pores. The higher number of hydrogen bonds present inside the pores is the reason for this [206,209]. Thanks to its higher water permeability than the best RO membranes currently in use, Tanugi and Grossman [209] determined that the new membrane for water treatment was superior to traditional RO membranes. Furthermore, two considerations influenced salt removal, strain, and pore size. It was discovered that aggregating the pore size and strain reduces the membrane's salt removal capacity. GR is another carbon allotrope that can be used in water desalination. This membrane has not yet been manufactured, but it is primarily based on simulation results; its promise is corresponding to that of nanoporous GR. The primary challenge for nanoporous GR membrane is direct proportionality of the amount of pores to water flow, and greater the number of pores allows the membrane's mechanical strength to deteriorate (Fig. 14 ). Pore scattering adds to the complexity of this technique, and mass fabrication of GR sheets must be lectured through thorough study [209,218].

In recent years, many metal oxide nanoparticles are combined with carbonaceous electron scavenger materials to grip over the shortcomings and growth of photocatalytic response, as presented in Fig. 15 . For illustration, in recent twenty years, numerous research articles have been conveyed on the photocatalytic response of GR-based materials. One characteristic of the growth and employment of GR-based systems is because of their unique properties such as economical, harmless nature, easily available and processable, obstructing corrosion and leaching, and dropping the combination of nanoparticles. Furthermore, it can be altered with numerous surfactants to boost the adsorption efficacy [4].

IUPAC is one of the standards, describing catalyst as that substance increasing rate of chemical reactions without modifying entire standards related with Gibbs energy variation in that reaction; hence process becomes catalysis [51]. Furthermore, photocatalysis refers to catalyst reactions that are thought to be dependent on, particularly, light energy and are carried out using a special setup (Fig. 16 ). Photocatalyst types depend on the catalyst phase, and catalysts in the same reactant phase are defined as homogeneous, while heterogeneous phase performance is known. Special steps with heterogeneous photocatalysis are used to develop a series of reactions that mainly eliminate organic aqueous impurities [220]. GR showing zero bandgap acts as a semimetal, presenting light absorbance ranging large wavelengths that become efficiently helpful toward photocatalysis [221,222]. GR containing few layers may play an active role as semiconductors that are considered better photocatalysts. Heterogeneous photocatalysis mechanism involves electrons excitation associated with photon energy from valence band (VB) toward CB along with holes formation in VB caused by incident light energy that becomes greater than bandgap of semiconducting materials [223]. Resultantly, electrons, as well as holes, carry on redox reactions becoming responsible for photocatalytic mechanisms. These redox reactions result in the formation of various types of radicals/intermediates like hydroxyl radicals serve as effective oxidizers on the surface of the photocatalyst and are hence commonly involved to remove wastewater organic contaminants containing dyes as well as other removable organic entities (Fig. 16a) [47]. It is deemed important for the provision of sufficient reactants for which charge carriers are assumed to be employed rather than interfering with the aquatic ecosystem since radical production created by them is seen to be highly active in nature.

As photocatalysis requires adsorbent presence for the provision of active sites of chemical reactions, GR is considered suitable for achieving this goal, being a highly efficient adsorbent, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity [3]. Therefore, TiO2 proved to be a good photocatalyst possessing promising photocatalytic properties, first time revealed and published by Akira Fujishima et al. since 1972 [224]. TiO2/GR photocatalytic nanocomposites exhibited versatile photocatalytic property that was widely researched as well as cataloged successfully [225–227]. UV irradiation along with visible irradiation presented enhanced photocatalytic activity related to TiO2/GR nanocomposites [228]. Efficiently enhanced catalytic activity was caused by CB electrons transfer of TiO2 toward GR, thereby enlarging bandgap whereas decreasing electron-hole pairs recombination that may occur in TiO2 (Fig. 15a) [229]. GR was found to be unique and effective in the eradication of RhB has thermodynamic properties. Furthermore, GR acted as an electron mediator in composite forming associated with SnO2, while in TiO2 nanocomposites, electrons trend in the form of freely motion into GR sheets or may readily interact with oxygen functional groups [230].

Further, similar behavior has been observed in Au/GR photocatalytic composite. However, 2,4-Dicholorophenoxyacetic acid associated with TiO2/GR proved more efficient in degradation mechanism that was considered four times more efficient, whereas dodecyl benzenesulfonate showed three times improved performance as compared with Degussa P25(commercial TiO2) where GR was reasonably doped by platinum to form GR/TiO2 nanocomposites [231,232]. Enhanced organic entities degradation may have been attributed to effective adsorbing of GR where TiO2 charge transfer occurred toward GR, whereas titanium bridged bond toward carbon associated with oxygen resulted with decrement bandgap value was observed [233]. P25-GR was found to be more effective than single Degussa P25 in eliminating Reactive Black 5 when exposed to UV light. Above 90 percent pollutant oxidation was successfully tested for a 100-minute irradiation period with UV belonging to a high surface region with a narrow bandgap [234]. Flowering G/titania sheets (G/TNS) were created using a hydrothermal process that efficiently produced Ti crystalline phases containing anatase as well as sodium titanate. GR, acting as a basis for Ti growth and reserving TiO2 growth alongside degraded 4-chlorophenol, demonstrated performance of greater than 99.2% over a 120-minute degradation time [235]. GR uses enhanced Ti photocatalytic performance owing to two logical reasons: firstly, enhancing transfer rate attributed to photo-excited electrons by hindrance of electron-hole pairs recombination; and secondly, narrowing bandgap values that result in efficient enhancement towards visible light absorption spectrum as demonstrated in Fig. 17a, b. The adsorption of the dye onto GO [236] increased as the pH was raised, Fig. 17d shows how this works. Hydroxyl radicals formed were observed such as reactive oxygen species that exhibited 4-chlorophenol degradation verifying via density functional theory (DFT) measurements and retained their effective role rather with the passage of further five cycles [51,235].

The Schottky barrier formation concept has been extensively studied in pre-reports [237,238]. Generally, individual metals, as well as n-type semiconductors, occupy a variety of Fermi levels owing to ϕ m and ϕ S as shown in Fig. 17c(i) respectively. If semiconductor and metal (in case of ϕ m> ϕ s) are electrically joined can be seen in Fig. 17c(ii), already resided electrons will quickly transfer from semiconductor CB having greater work function towards metal showing smaller work function continue till both aligned Fermi energy levels are evaluated, thereby encountering charge layer formation at interfacial electrical-contact. Resultantly, metal surface excess negative charge layers are formed, whereas electron transfer from barrier region produces a layer of excess positive charges relevant to semiconductors, thereby forming upward band-bending towards the surface. Subsequently, a narrow barrier created at depleted layer space is regarded as Schottky barrier, and its height denoted by ϕ m, is calculated by the following formula: ∅ b =∅ m − X
s

Where, Xs is electron affinity, measured from CB-edge towards vacuum level corresponding to semiconductor. The role of Schottky barrier presents efficient electron trapping, thereby creating reasonable partial electron-density for co-catalysts forming photocatalysis whereas preventing unexpected electrons transfer from co-catalysts back towards semiconductors. In general, noble metals are those showing greater work-function associated with n-type semiconductors to create Schottky barrier, therefore attaining better charge split-up along with the enhanced photocatalytic activity. At this stage, every strategy enlarging GR work function is considered more beneficial to enlarge Schottky barrier height among GR as well as n-type semiconductors, thereby resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance

Generally, two strategies may improve electrical conductivity and may also enlarge GR work function creating suitable Schottky heterojunctions (HJs) via increasing specific Schottky barriers. Among efficient strategies, reduction in GR defect sites is that one which may improve GR conductivity, thus introducing a very conductive substance. At this level, solvent-exfoliated graphene (SEG) is suggested best as compared with rGO for forming GR derivative Schottky junctions with high quality [240–242]. As an introduction, Xu and coworkers [241] produced a sequence attributing CdS/GR (rGO and SEG) Schottky HJs by employing favorable one-step solvothermal strategy. Novelty exhibiting SEG/CdS Schottky HJs linked with minimum GR defect densities present superior activities relevant to alcoholic-aerobic selective-oxidation (Fig. 18b) and removal of heavy ions Cr(VI) (Fig. 18a, c) as compared with rGO/TiO2 photocatalytic nanocomposite. It has been suggested that reserved CdS photo corrosion with efficient charge separation occurring in Schottky HJs are related to enhanced photocatalytic activity in reversible redox reactions. Furthermore, some areas of research such as charge kinetics, work function variance lying between SEG and rGO as well as improved reactant adsorption have not been discussed in this study, as this deficiency should be improved with full concentration incoming research studies. Moreover, introducing efficient conductive-polymers, Polyaniline as an example, may also be employed to enhance electrical conductivity attributed with GR nanosheets, leading to more advantageous toward formation corresponding to superior quality Schottky HJs [243–245]

Another efficient strategy improving electrical conductivity as well as increasing work function attributing GR is suggested as heteroatoms such as (O, N, B, P, and S) doping process [246]. Generally, pyridinic N2-doped GR may result in creating maximum density of states towards Fermi level whereas also reducing work function [247,248], on the other hand, relative electronegativity corresponding to graphitic N atoms decreases electron density on the adjacent boundary of carbon nuclei, that may cause transfer of electrons from adjacent carbon toward N atoms, whereas N-back provides electrons to adjacent carbon Pz-orbitals. Furthermore, graphitic N performs an active role as an effective promoter towards oxygen reduction reaction activity, rather than pyridinic-N, caused by aided O2 dissociation on carbon atoms as well as a strong chemical bond among O and carbon atoms [247]. Promoted N2-GR carrier density enhances electrical conductivity creating Schottky junctions, thereby retarding electron-hole pair recombination, so that photo activity, as well as photostability, may significantly promote photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalysts [249–253]. More significantly, three important N2-containing groups are possessing a variety of properties, such as graphitic N, pyrrolic N as well as pyridinic N inherited with various other functions owing to a wide area of photocatalytic applications [254]. Similarly, it has also been evaluated that graphitic-N added in N-doped GR/Fe2O3, corresponding to Schottky junctions show vital roles while stimulating transfer along with transportation attributed to photo-created charges and developing adsorption phenomena owing to CO2 and O2 [255]. Consequently, an attractive and synergistic effect may obtain prominently improved photocatalytic activity toward selective reduction of CO2 to CO along with successful acetaldehyde degradation. Moreover, synergistic effect relevant to N-doped TiO2 as well as N-doped GR may enhance photocatalytic activity related to H2 production performance owing to commercial P25 with 13.1-factor effect [256]. Consequently, the main focus should be towards positive synergistic effect corresponding to variety of modified strategies, thereby maximizing efficient photocatalytic activity. In some other research work, N-doped GR composite (N/rGO) synthesized through GO calcination in ammonia(NH3) atmosphere was integrated with CdS by employing facile two-step synthesis approach exhibiting solution deposition associated with calcination in N2 environment [257]. Han et al. [258] have studied CdS nanowires-N2-doped GR (CdS NWs-NGR) Schottky HJs fabrication process via electrostatic self-assembly approach adopted by hydrothermal process (Fig. 18d, e)

N-doped GR was incorporated with a variety of p-type semiconductors; ZnSe, as well as MoS2, synthesized GR-based p-n HJs [249,259–261]. As an example, N-doped RGO/ZnSe p-n HJs have been prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal strategy at slow temperature [249]. ZnSe nanorods combined with ZnSe nanoparticles were evaluated for depositing on N-doped GR sheet’s surface (Fig. 19a). It was observed that N-rGO/ZnSe p-n HJs exhibited effective photocatalytic performance corresponding to methyl orange degradation activity irradiated by visible light (Fig. 19b). It was believed that developed photocatalytic activity may be ascribed to greater adsorption related to contaminant molecules, and dominantly improved light irradiation absorption (Fig. 19c). Moreover, it was enhanced charge transportation as well as separation owing to the occurrence of N/rGO-ZnSe p-n HJs. Besides these developed versatile GR-based p-n HJs, ideal Type-II p-n HJs have been cited in recent decades, caused by photoelectrons residing in GR-based semiconductors that may migrate towards wide bandgap semiconductors other than irradiation, thus presenting extended time for electrons, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity

For instance, Feng et al. [262] have offered work in favor of alkylated GR quantum dots (AGQDs)/TiO2 nano-photocatalysts synthesized via facile ex-situ strategy showing superior photodegradation rate in the visible region towards RhB as compared with intrinsic TiO2 and AGQDs (Fig. 19d). In addition, the AGQDs-TiO2 spectrum exhibits greater enhanced light absorption, especially in visible light region (400-700 nm) (Fig. 19e). Developed photocatalytic performance attributing AGQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite may be identified with collective effects corresponding to photosensitization of AGQDs, and an ideal Type-II HJ, depicted in Fig. 19f, Xu et al. [263] have evaluated photosensitization role of GR in the formation of GR/ZnO nanocomposites by employing irradiation process under visible light in the form of Cr(VI) photoreduction in aqueous media. Wavelength-directed performance indicates that photoactivity reduction rate of Cr(VI) becomes valuable by increasing the irradiated energy of visible light. Resultantly, numerous electrons transferring from ground state GR toward high excited energy level (GR*) may have promoted photo excitation level via greater irradiated energy, leading to inject into CB belonging to ZnO by unique behavior of Type-II HJ, in turn leading to significant photocatalytic activity in reduction Cr(VI)

Conventionally, all-solid-state (ASS) Z-scheme photocatalysts are classified into direct and indirect systems, as shown in Fig. 20a, b. Traditionally, all-solid-state (ASS, Z-scheme) photocatalysts are more suitable for application in solid and gas phases without shuttle redox mediators. Particularly, ASS (Z-scheme) photocatalysts may control some problems in first-generation liquid phase photocatalysts such as backward and competing reactions among mediator and reactants [237,264,265]. However, ASS (Z-scheme) photocatalysts attained more attraction in the field of photocatalysis

To build indirect HJs, semi-metallic GR might be utilized as a progressive solid electron mediator. In 2006, Tada et al. [266] first suggested the idea of second-generation photocatalysts, ASS Z-scheme (CdS-Au-TiO2) nano junction photocatalytic systems. The indirect Z-scheme systems were broadly and efficaciously engineered with noble metals inclusive of gold and platinum treated as stable interfacial mediators. Therefore, the noble metal along with their significant light absorbers highly bound the broad applications of indirect Z-scheme photocatalysts. Favorably, earth's considerable conductive rGO mediators deliver young opportunities for building robust indirect Z-scheme for photocatalytic programs. In particular, rGO mediators can improve reactants' adsorption and visible light absorption. Therefore, it is not surprising that in several past years, GR-based indirect Z-scheme photocatalysts have attained much attraction

For instance, through rGO acted as a solid shuttle redox mediator; Amal and his coworkers [267] have designed interesting indirect ASS photocatalysts. The photogenerated particles in BiVO4 might be inserted from its CB to rGO nanosheets and after that rejoin to photo-generated holes in (Ru/SrTiO3). Subsequently, remaining electrons in (Ru/SrTiO3): Rh can drive the hydrogen evolution reaction over (Ru) co-catalyst, whereas holes in BiVO4 would drive water oxidation contact, achieving an overall complete water splitting. Particularly, as compared with ionic (Fe+3/Fe+2 and IO3 −/I−) redox couples in typical Z-scheme systems, rGO redox mediator reveals several unique benefits, involving simple recovery reusability and avoiding unexpected surface back reactions. In a recent, it has been elucidated that rGO solid mediator might be applied to build Z-scheme photocatalytic systems among p-type metal sulfide semiconductors (CuGaS2, CuInS2, Cu2ZnGeS2, and Cu2ZnSnS2) [268] and n-type Ti, owing to effective transfer charge carriers in Ti to metal sulfides using a (p-type) semiconductor nature (Fig. 20c). Ideal Pt/CuGaS2-TiO2 composite taking as an example, the incorporation of rGO can enhance photocatalytic response and attain hydrogen and oxygen generation in stoichiometric quantities (Fig. 20d, e). Thus, intrinsic CuGaS2 photo corrosion will rather lower hydrogen and oxygen evolution rate by increasing irradiation time, which must be more enhanced in near future researches. Further indirect ASS (Z-scheme) systems, for instance (ZnO/rGO/CdS) [269], could also be utilized in many photocatalytic systems. Remarkably, in constructing these structures, a few important factors, such as similar geometric and electric band structure, semiconductors in crystalline and microscopic shape, as well as rGO electrical conductivity need to be followed wisely to attain high photocatalysis performance

Conversely, semiconducting GR is promising for engineering direct Z-scheme photocatalysts. Accurately, direct Z-scheme photocatalytic systems show several advantages over traditional HJ, liquid-phase, and ASS (Z-scheme) photocatalysts, specifically, economical construction, less charge recombination paths and minimized light-shielding effect, which renders them a potential candidate for many photocatalysis reactions [265,270]. As it has been described before, GO is a semiconducting material having a 2.5 eV bandgap, with CB and VB ranges (-0.75 - 1.75 eV) vs. NHE, correspondingly [271,272]. However, GO as a significant semiconductor contains much attention for practical application. Xu et al. [273] for instance elucidated that Ag2CrO4-GO Z-scheme HJ revealed exceptional photocatalytic reaction and stability towards dyes and phenol degradation in water under irradiation of visible light as shown in Fig. 20f. Through loading optimal content of GO (1.0 wt%), a 3.5-fold improvement in photocatalytic activity of pure Ag2CrO4 particles can be obtained. The increased photocatalytic reaction is especially ascribed to the fabrication of Ag2CrO4-GO HJ that could not only assist separation and transportation of photo-excited electrons, however, also conserves robust redox capability as indicated in Fig. 20g. Enhanced photostability is owing to effective reticence of Ag2CrO4 photo-corrosion through transferring photo-excited electrons of Ag2CrO4 to VB of GO. Later, Min et al. [274] elucidated a Z-scheme photocatalyst with Ag@AgCl enclosed using GO, where GO and AgCl behave like activated photocatalysts, and Ag-metallic shuttles photo-excited electrons from excited GO and AgCl under UV light irradiation

The ensuing Z-scheme photogenerated system showed a considerable photocatalytic response compared to (Ag@AgCl) rGO through equal quantitative GR (15 wt%) loading for MB degradation, thus affirming the role of GR as semiconductor. In the same text, Ebrahimi et al. [275] engineered a direct Z-scheme GR quantum dots (GQD)/ZnO nanowires. The synthesized GQD/ZnO NWs discovered a particularly stepped forward photocatalytic degradation of MB under light irradiation, owing to more effective charge carrier generation; transfer and separation depend on various radical trapping experiments (Fig. 21a). Remarkably, Song et al. [276] successfully fabricated the direct Z-scheme Ag2CO3-N-doped GR photocatalytic system with NG as co-catalyst (Fig. 21b) displayed considerably higher photocatalytic performance towards phenol contaminant than Ag2CO3 and its nanocomposites with GO or rGO as co-catalyst. It has been discovered that plasmonic Ag nanoparticles (NPs) can be created on Ag2CO3-N-doped GR owing to natural decrease of Ag+ through pyridinic nitrogen species (N-p) of N-doped GR, while graphitic nitrogen species (Ng) may increase proficiency of photo-excited charge split-up, Z-scheme transferring ability, and O2 up take. Hence, the as-obtained direct Z-scheme photocatalysis offer a significant candidate for photocatalytic applications. In coming future, it is considered that additional direct Z-scheme photocatalysis for depletion of pollutants and solar fuel generation might be fabricated using direct economical GO semiconductor

In recent times, it was exhibited that fabrication of self-controlled Van der Waals (vdW) HJs can be engineered and recognize distinct optoelectronic devices are better than traditional bulk counterparts [277]. Intriguingly, 2D-2D vdW p-n HJs were easily engineered by setting down (p-type) MoS2 nanoplatelets on (n-type), nitrogen-based-rGO semiconductor nanosheets (as indicated in Fig. 22a, b) [261]. In earth crust the most abundantly found p-type MoS2 and n-type rGO vdW heterostructures exhibit substantially increased photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity in the range of wavelength from UV to near-IR, attributable to increased charge production and inhibited charge recombination (Fig. 22c). Remarkably, it was elucidated that sheet/rod sandwich such as the structural design with CdS nanorods surrounding a vdW p-n junction of (MoS2/N-rGO), like increasing active edges for hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions, and assisting spatial charge separation to contrary with their inner electric field [278]

Depending on DFT studies, Fu et al. [279] described 2D vdW (MoSe2/GR/HfS2 and MoSe2/N-doped GR/HfS2), HJs are suggested to be favorable candidates for Z-scheme photocatalysts. The sufficient monitoring of (n-type) doping on GR could increase solar energy utilization performance. In addition, 2D vdW (MoSe2/HfS2) vdW HJ is displayed to be a direct Z-scheme system for water splitting photocatalytic in the absence of redox mediators would be synthesized very easily (Fig. 22d-f). This work is much favorable for a profound understanding of theoretical construction of 2D vdW heterojunctions. Iqbal et al. [280] more recently first described that hydrogen bubbles produced by photocatalytic water splitting are sufficient to exfoliate layer-by-layer MoS2 nanocrystals into a few layers. The in situ joined few-layered MoS2/CdS nanosheets depend on vdW HJs, is described to be capable of charge transfer and separation, for this reason suggesting to hydrogen elimination rate of 140 mmol g/(CdS)/h through obtained yield of 66% at 420 nm [280]. Now GR/CdS nanosheets depend on vdW HJs are predicted for wastewater applications. These findings further asserted that distinct 2D-2D vdW HJs could be beneficial for collecting solar energy and electron transfer to reaction edges as compared to other 0D-2D and 1D-2D hybrid networks

Laterally stacked HJs or different forms of layer-by-layer, in-plane HJs through surprising properties offer more efficient charge split-up and exhibited active edges for photocatalytic response over 2D nanosheets. For instance, at low temperature with vapor growth, smooth and single atom in-plane WS2/MoS2 HJs can be fabricated with lateral WS2 epitaxy on MoS2 edges [281] can be created efficient localized photoluminescence improvement and pure p-n junctions. Theoretically, in-plane heterostructure stands for a strong promising response in photocatalysis. Intriguingly, Teng’s group engineered N-doped GO QDs using ammoniation process of GO at 500 oC, which displayed n and p-type conductivities presented by electrochemical Mott-Schottky phenomena [282]. Significantly, it was considered that these N-doped GO QDs can attain complete water splitting using an H2:O2 ratio of (2:1) under visible-light irradiation (420 nm < λ < 800 nm, see Fig. 23a, b). The synthesis of an in-plane internal Z-scheme heterostructure, containing functional groups of oxygen and N-doped GO possessing types of (p- and n-type) conductivity is attributed for hydrogen photocatalysis and oxygen elimination respectively (Fig. 23b). Moreover, this team of researchers also described concurrence of n- and p-domain in surface interaction N-doped GO quantum dots fabricated with ultrasonic exfoliation of (NH3-treated) GO nanosheets can also attain in-plane Z-scheme heterostructures in helping charge separation and transport, which leading significantly increased H2 photocatalytic production in triethanolamine liquid phase [283]. So as a whole complete metal-free candidate, N-doped quantum dots comprise in-plane Z-scheme heterostructures that are found more effective in different photocatalysis justifies further attraction for imminent researches

In the current past, Che et al. [284] used a scheme of thermal conjugate of sp2-hybridized (C-N) bonds between carbon ring glucose substance and melem unit and suggested an evident structure of distinct 2D plane HJ carbon ring (Cring)-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 23c, d). In this structure, local conjugated electric field can enhance light absorption synchronously; boost separation of electron-hole pair and transfer of photoelectron, thus lifetime and transmission of photoelectrons are enhanced by 10 folds than bare g-C3N4 (Fig. 23e). Amazingly, in-plane (Cring)-C3N4 heterostructures can receive a definite rate of production (371 mol g-1 h-1) of hydrogen with 5% remarkable yield at 420 nm for splitting of pure water efficiently under light irradiation (Fig. 23f). Characterization of electronic configuration and DFT observations exhibited that substantial electron density transportation close to (Cring)-C3N4 Fermi level in-plane heterostructures is the critical element for increased photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and transportation and augmented surface kinetics response, therefore attaining enhanced photocarrier application. The consequences may cover new and economical research methods for metal-free in-plane heterostructures for different photocatalysis

Generally, devising multicomponent HJs can achieve concurrently enhanced visible light utilization, improved charge carriers’ transportation, and surface photocatalytic response. Specifically, the engineered various interfacial electrical fields in HJs multicomponent can develop the transportation/separation of charges significantly and thus obtain significantly boosted photocatalytic performance. Conventionally, by combining two junctions e.g. Schottky junctions and Type-II HJs (or Z-scheme HJs) might be attained two-step separation charge carriers, by which lifetime of charges increases and hence directing to wide photocatalytic applications. For instance, Yue and his coworkers [285] successfully engineered CdS/Nb2O5/N-doped-GO multicomponent HJs, initially, charge carriers move from CdS to Nb2O5 CB through Type-II CdS/Nb2O5 HJs; then through Schottky junctions charges transfer to the surface of N-doped GO nanosheets, enhancing the electron-hole pair separation significantly. The maximum H2-elimination photocatalytic ratio of engineered CdS/Nb2O5/N-doped-GO HJs multicomponent is 7.7 and 3.5-times greater than pure CdS and CdS/Nb2O5, respectively. Therefore, this research offers a better understanding of fabricated noble metal-free friendly atmosphere photocatalysis H2 production under visible light irradiation. In the same context, Type-II g-C3N4/TiO2 with (001 vs 101) facets/rGO [286,287], (CdS/ZnO), [288,289], polyaniline/Cu2O and TiO2 [243,244], CuZnO@Fe3O4, (CuO/TiO2) [290], ZnO/ZnS and ZnS-Ag2S [291] HJs were also combined with rGO to generate multi-component showed efficient increased photocatalytic towards photo-degeneration of organic and photo-reduction of carbon dioxide to methanol, respectively, owing to increase in charge transportation and surface photocatalytic response. However, N-doped GR was also extensively used to favor Type-II semiconductor HJs. For instance, Li et al. [292] fabricated AgBr NPs for the first time reinforced on g-C3N4-decorated N-GR (NG) HJs multicomponent (ACNNG-x), which exhibited potential efficacy for catalytic depletion of organic pollutant and reduction of carbon dioxide under visible light. This type of unique (ACNNG-x) multicomponent HJs can realize improved charge transportation efficiency, robust redox tendency, and long-term stability. Further complete photocatalytic phenomena of these (ACNNG-x) HJ multi-components have been suggested and discussed comprehensively (Fig. 24 )

Remarkably, this research shows a green and convenient scheme to obtain an increased solar photocatalytic reaction and offers young insights for designing and production of new higher proficiency HJ multicomponent photocatalysts. Wang et al. [293] successfully synthesized a distinct CeO2 (nanorods/g-C3N4/N-rGO) multicomponent HJs through a simple approach i.e. ultrasonic-heat treatment that revealed superior reaction and stability for RhB dissociation. RbB exhibits a 2.1-times higher photodegradation rate as compared to (g-C3N4/N-rGO) in the presence of N-rGO catalyst (2.5 wt%) when CeO2 nanorod’s concentration was about 2 wt%. This increment in degradation rate is attributed to the efficient charge transportation, reduction of O2, and reactant adsorption in (CeO2/g-C3N4/N-rGO) HJ multicomponent. Moreover, superoxide anion radical (•O2 −) and cations were described to be core items in photodegradation method. The present study introduced a deep understanding of multi-functional roles of N-doped GR in increasing Type-II HJs activity. More excitingly, Wan et al. [294] designed a distinct GO enclosed [Ag2SO3/AgBr] (GO/Ag2SO3/AgBr) HJ multicomponent with a simple solution method through electrostatic contact and precipitation transformation response. The results elucidated that dopant amount of GO nanosheets can considerably increase photocatalytic and recyclability activity of (Ag2SO3/AgBr) NPs. It is reported that a plasmonic Z-scheme HJ favored on rGO might be employed to elucidate the probable mechanism of transported and separated performance of charge carriers amongst (Ag, Ag2SO3, AgBr, and GO) quaternary system under UV irradiation.
