Briefly speaking, fabricating rGO favored (Type-II or Z-scheme HJs) must be a potential approach to increase photocatalytic activity contains more interest in near-future researches. Moreover, HJ multi-components by combining GR-based Schottky HJs and GR/co-catalyst HJs were also constructed broadly, which can not only boost the departure of electron-hole in semiconductors through electron transfer and large electrical conductivity of GR, rather increase surface electrocatalytic response over co-catalysts owing to augmented electrons in GR [295], hence obtaining highly enriched photocatalytic performance. Xiang et al. [296] for instance, engineered a young GR-based HJ multicomponent comprising of CdS nanorods developed on categorized WS2/GR layered photocatalyst. The optimal ternary (CdS/WS2/GR) HJ multi-components can offer largest vis--light photocatalysis H2 production ratio of (1842 mol h-1g-1) using an apparent significant proficiency of (21.2%) at wavelength of 420 nm. The ternary (WS2-CdS/rGO) multicomponent HJs may be effectively improved charge separation and interfacial transfer of charges and offer more active edges for increased photocatalytic H2-elimination. The ternary (WS2-CdS/rGO) multicomponent HJs could improve separation and interfacial transfer of charges more efficiently and consequently elevate photocatalytic elimination of H2 (Fig. 25a)

Yan et al. [297] synthesized [Ni(OH)2 doped CdS/rGO] multicomponent with (1.0 wt%) Ni(OH2) concentration and elucidated its 10-times greater efficiency for H2-production at the rate of (4731 mol h-1g-1) as compared to original CdS/rGO photocatalyst under similar circumstances it is recommended that separation method of photocatalytic electrons from CdS photo harvester to acceptor electron in rGO, then to vigorous Ni(OH2) co-catalyst, hence achieving highly enhanced photocatalytic H2-production (Fig. 25b). By coupling with several semiconductors, it is strongly recommended that distinct GR/co-catalyst HJs are to be exploited to engineer new improved multi-component with the same available HJs e.g. RGO/WS2/Mg-doped ZnO [298]. Intriguingly, the combination of distinct metal NPs surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) effect and GR-based Schottky HJ was found to be a potential approach to build multicomponent HJs for rapid photocatalytic performance for several applications [299]. Wang et al. [300], designed GR-doped (Au-TiO2) multicomponent HJs using a microwave-supported facile single-step hydrothermal route. The improved multi-component HJs can obtain the largest visible-light-driven hydrogen elimination rate of (296 molh-1g-1) with 4.1% quantum efficiency, as compared with original Ti and binary-compounds. It is considered that GR-based (Au-TiO2) multi-component HJs enhance Ti visible-light reaction and space transportation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, owing to effectively exploring SPR effect of Au NPs and fast electron transfer- functions of GR components (Fig. 25c, d). Furthermore, Low et al. [301] hydrothermally engineered a plasmonic (Ag-rGO-Bi2WO6) HJ multi-component using visible light, chemical redox method was adopted in the presence of ethyl glycol (reducing agent), and three folds greater photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB than original Bi2WO6 was observed

The greater photoactivity is mainly attributed to constructed multicomponent among plasmonic Ag-NPs, strong-conductivity GR, and Bi2WO6 nanosheets, hence increasing formation and separation of excited electrons from Bi2WO6, and adsorption ability of RhB (Fig. 25e, f). Thus, the direction of extracted charges from plasmonic Ag-NPs, semiconductor to GR is needed to be further explored. Moreover, the quarternary HJs have been synthesized extensively through scrutinizing GR-based Schottky junctions, GR/co-catalyst, and Type II HJs [302,303]. For example, Tonda et al. [304] fabricated quarternary [g-C3N4(2D)/CdS(1D)/rGO(2D)/Pt(0D)] multicomponent HJs with a wet-chemical technique. Findings indicated that maximum hydrogen generation rate of (~ 4800 molh−1g−1) is almost 4411 and 2.5 times greater compared to real (g-C3N4) and (g-C3N4/CdS) photocatalysts, correspondingly. The exceptional photocatalytic activity could be ascribed to augmented departure/transportation of photo-induced charge carrier using appropriate p-n HJ among (g-C3N4 and CdS), rGO high-conductivity, and increased absorption of visible light and photostability, and minimum hydrogen elimination over-potential on Pt NPs. In future prospects, it is considered that multicomponent HJs can be increasingly fabricated and employed in several photocatalytic sectors. Significantly, interfacial properties in these HJ multicomponents photogenerated charge carrier dynamics must be carefully optimized

Carbon Quantum dot (CQDs) ranging 1.2-3.8 nm showing superior up-conversion photoluminescence properties have been primarily presented by Li et al. [306] by employing facile single-step alkali-assisted- electrochemical method. As prepared TiO2/CQDs, as well as SiO2/CQDs semiconducting photo composites, have been studied with highly efficient visible-light response toward the elimination of organic as well as inorganic dye particularly MB. High photodegradation performance of SiO2/CQDs (TiO2/CQDs) photo NPs towards MB contaminants was evaluated ~ 98% with 20 min irradiation associated with halogen-lamp of 300 W power while controlled results with intrinsic CQDs with or without metal oxide (SiO2/TiO2) component exhibited negligible MB (0%) degradation. A CQDs/ZnO photo nanocomposites ranging from 20nm to 30nm were prepared by employing hydrothermal one-step facile method [307], thereby utilized toward photodegradation identified with toxic-methanol and benzene (> 85% photodegradation activity in 24h) in air media irradiated with visible-light response at normal temperature

Synthesized nano-complex derivative photocatalysts such as WO3/CQDs, CQDs/BiOI) and NeZnO/CQDs revealed great contribution with promising proficiency with respect to photodegradation containing various organic pollutants [308–310]. Zhao et al. [311] have established fabrication as well as photocatalytic prowess relevant to CQDs associated with Bi2WO6 nanocomposite toward photocatalytic removal identified with gaseous volatile organic compounds by employing UV irradiation as well as visible-light; thereby broadening absorption of visible-light caused by improved photo excited career recombination/separation that revealed an improved photo-oxidation activities/stabilities attributed to toluene and acetone. On the same ground, Mendes et al. [312] have also described proficiency of NCQDs/TiO2 (P25) nanocomposites toward photo oxidation of nitrogen oxide (NO) irradiated through UV-Vis light, whereas NO variety augmented with ~10% (for P25) to ~27% (for N-CQDs/TiO2) as well as 58.5% to 79.5% under operation toward for UV and Visible light relatively

Among many candidates towards improved CQDs based photocatalysts, metal sulfides are ones such as CdS showing superior qualities, like narrow bandgap, excellent transport, and superior chemical/thermal stability. Thus, CQDs/CdS nano photocatalysts have been synthesized by Zhang and coworkers [313] by employing hydrothermal method along with applications in photodegradation regarding RhB has been evaluated, thereby CQDs may efficiently capture electrons resulting to decrease recombination corresponding to photo-excited carriers; ~50% RhB photodegradation efficiency was obtained through flowery CdS, whereas 1.1% CQDs/CdS may increase degradation-efficiency toward 90% performance. Photodegradation performance relevant to Alizarin red S (ARS) contaminant was carried out utilizing CQDs/ZnS photo nanocatalysts by Kansal et al. [314], where demonstrating high photocatalytic action toward ARS ~90% reduction after 250 minutes, proved to be 1.5 times greater performance of pristine ZnS (~64%) in visible-light irradiated time. Polymer dots (PDs) was also prepared from efficient polymer polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) core decorated carbon dots (CDs) [315] that were treated hydrothermally for PVA precursor [316]. CDs/PDs-TiO2 NPs unified with Ti-O-C bands were prepared via grafting CDs/PDs decorated TiO2 NPs through employing hydrothermal management, thereby ensuing semiconductor/CD nano-hybrid-composites were evaluated to examine photoreduction of MO dye in aqueous media (Fig. 26a). Synergetic behavior of CDs/PDs as well as TiO2 may efficiently improve MO toxic removal, whereas the photocatalytic rate constant attributed to PDs/TiO2 was 9.5 as well as 3.6 times better than commercial intrinsic P25 and TiO2 respectively (Fig. 26b, c)

The newly fabricated hybrid nanocomposites containing CQDs as well as inorganic NPs such ZnO, Fe2O3, and silica were accomplished. Zhang et al. [317] synthesized by employing hydrothermal approach (Fig. 27a), Fe2O3/CQDs nanocomposites indicating efficient photocatalysts towards photodegradation relevant to poisonous gases such as benzene/methanol under visible irradiated conditions; However, routes followed in favor of Fe2O3/CQDs hybrids along with cubic morphology related with magnetic architecture as well as versatile photochemical applications, with various morphologies as illustrated in Fig. 27b-e. In the form of CQDs nature, magnetic nano-hybrids may unify magnetic response associated with fluorescence adopting as same routes. CQD occupy a high electron cloud and photon excited electrons extracted from Fe2O3 NPs travel efficiently towards CQD conducting system, depicted in Fig. 28a. Adsorbents like O2 and OH and electron-hole pairs quickly interact with each other, exhibiting a large number of active oxygen radicals owing to strong oxidation ability to remove noxious gases

Furthermore, newly created nanohybrids may actively associate fluorescence characteristics of CQDs with properties such as magnetic, optical, and mechanical towards oxide’s core formation within core-shell fluorescent or magnetic photocatalysts [306,318,319], indicating iron oxide core attached with CQDs through covalent bonding due to surface functional groups associated with nanohybrids species and/or with electrostatic interactions showing electrostatically charged shells towards nanohybrid species. In this regard, core-shell and/or fluorescent-photocatalysts utilizing shells extracted from CQDs associated with numerous NP cores such as biogenic magnetite, CNTs have been fabricated by Markova et al. [318] via a new useful protocol to modify negatively charged NP cores using CQDs as well as butane hydrochloride, electrostatically interacting (Fig. 28b-f) and showing core-shell architecture through coating magnetite as well as CNTs with the incorporation of CQDs. CQDs were deployed on the same grounds as photocatalytic activities were employed in environmental remediation through removal of organic pollutants like azo-dyes and/or volatile organic compounds. A variety of techniques were emerged to enrich photocatalytic performance owing to semiconductor CQDs derivatives photocatalysts, comprising loading of metals as well as metal oxides, designed structure, and formation of semiconductors nanocomposites and/or hybrids [246,320–322]. Numerous newly designed CQDs derived photo/nanocatalysts, containing GQDs associated with TiO2 [323,324], Fe3O4 [325], MnNb2O6 [326], and ZnO [327], graphitic-carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) [323,328], and/or also NeBi2O2CO3 [329], were designed in favor of visible light as well as NIR-driven irradiated towards photodegradation

In this work, modified GQDs mesoporous-C3N4 HJs may have been fabricated employing an electrostatic interaction [330], whereas nanocomposites may architect large superoxide radicals along with holes towards active photodegradation owing RhB as well as tetracycline, as shown in Fig. 28g. On the same grounds, Liu et al. [331] reported GQD/zinc porphyrin HJ, thereby examining proficiency towards rapid decomposition belonging aqueous MB towards visible light irradiated photodegradation process. GQDs have been introduced for aryl basal flat architecture associated with efficient carboxylic groups as compared with microstructured GO layers [332,333]. More precisely, GQDs exhibited superior inherent peroxidase prowess than that of micro-sized GO, in turn improving performance with stability while detecting H2O2. Zhang et al. [318] have reported in favor of Au NPs/GQDs nanohybrids, that they are synergetic/switchable peroxidase nature efficiency for H2O2 disintegration along with H2O2 identification in respect of physiology and/or pathology. Likewise, nanocomposites containing GQDs as well as Fe3O4 NPs, being synergistic peroxidase nano catalytic systems, were reported [325], whereas they may be formed by employing a single step co-precipitation method removing phenolic compounds. Synthesized GQDs/Fe3O4 nanocomposites possessing weight ratio (1:1) in favor of H2O2 have supported oxidation levels of 3,30,5,50-tetramethylbenzidine, demonstrating comparable and/or superior catalytic activities, as compared with unique Fe3O4 NPs (~ 22 times), GQDs (~ 25 times), whereas GO/Fe3O4 NPs (~ 9 times) folded architectures respectively

Moreover, the combined effect of Fe3O4/NPs through Fe-O is chemically linked, resulting in electron transfer from electron comprised GQDs towards magnetic NPs, harvesting large ions of Fe(II), playing a vital role in favor of peroxidase nature nano catalytic performances owing Fe3O4 NPs. N2-doping is amongst novel strategies modifying as well as modulating structural modifications, chemically and electronically featured GR and GQDs [334,335] as evaluated in preparation regarding N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites while employing hydrothermal route, whereas its deployment being photo-nano-catalyst towards photodegradation owing to aqueous MB [336] N-GQDs presented dominant effect in favor of photocatalytic efficiency relevant to TiO2 NPs showing 40% to 85% result for MB photodegradation irradiated with UV-light. The latest study [337] has demonstrated photocatalytic performance regarding N-GQDs-BiVO4@C3N4-Z schematic hetero-junction structures towards photodegradation for antibiotic pollutants, as an example, oxytetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and tetracycline was evaluated as higher as compared with BiVO4@C3N4; as preparing HJs and exhibiting unique photocatalytic performance of 92% tetracycline photodegradation during 30 minutes. In addition, combination of S-N-co-doped-GQDs with g-C3N4 has been synthesized showing perfect nature photo/nanocatalyst towards photodegradation of impurities [338] whereas fabricated S, N-GQD/g-C3N4 (with size 6-10 nm) nanocomposites have discovered superior photocatalytic efficiency while removing RhB dye under visible light conditions than that of intrinsic g-C3N4 obeying identical conditions. Consequently, enhanced degradation behavior was under reasonable photogenerated electron-hole separation in S, N-GQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. The hydrothermal route is regarded best one amongst afore-mentioned routes showing a greener-effective approach [339–344]. Furthermore, natural bio-wastes and/or bio-sources have been efficiently utilized offering green environment, low-budget based whereas ample in availability owing carbon sources for the production of desired photocatalysts

The g-C3N4 as a non-metal photocatalyst exhibits significant potential to resolve environmental remediation and energy calamity because of distinct optical properties, economical, and easy availability [345–349]. Indeed, the active site's effect on photocatalytic response of g-C3N4 is unquestionable. At a molecular level, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) in g-C3N4 frameworks is mostly made of N2 orbitals, whereas N2, N3, C1, and C2 form lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) as shown in Fig. 29a [264]. Consequently, N atoms provide oxidation active regions, and C atoms provide reduction active edges in photocatalytic activity [350]. Therefore, separation of these two types of atoms for redox reaction is a challenge possesses delocalized-conjugated structure related to its chemical bonding, which ultimately reduces the active edges [351]. Traditionally speaking, at a macro level, a photocatalyst with high specific surface area mainly possesses several active sites. Thus, the specific surface area is not only a single element that affects active sites. A rich photocatalytic scheme of inactive sites must require engineering completely and precisely with basal and hybrid engineering from breath and lengthwise perspective. Traditionally, via calcination of N2-consisting photocatalysts, g-C3N4 was designed in stacked bulk structure possessing very small specific surface area which in turn further reduces active edges on its surface [352–355]. Moreover, all factors, including reduced surface area, passive surface materials, and less surface adsorption ability, are collectively responsible for the reduction of mass and number of active sites in the conventional g-C3N4 and render it a moderate photocatalyst. In order to improve photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4, the number of active edges on the surface of conventional g-C3N4 must be increased. In this context, basal and hybrid engineering are fruitful techniques for improving carbon-nitride active sites in surface, migration of charges, substrate absorption ability, and other surface treatments. These features lead to structure and surface morphology of basal engineering, doping defects, loading of co-catalyst, and nanostructured semiconductor of hybrid engineering. Thus by regulating both engineering i.e. basal and hybrid, it is proved a great technique to enhance g-C3N4 active sites. Fig. 29a shows distribution of redox-active sites in g-C3N4 bases at a molecular level. Till now, the link between catalytic response and active sites has been studied broadly [345,356–358]. By adapting quantity and quality of these active sites, photocatalytic activity can be directly determined [359]

Since Wang et al. [350] first applied g-C3N4 in visible-light photocatalysis for the generation of H2, several g-C3N4-based photocatalysts were produced. Moreover, many substances engineering strategies were developed to boost photocatalytic response of g-C3N4. Zhang et al. [360] demonstrated a facile and economical route to enhance photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. They have used sacrificial models to generate pores inside the bulk g-C3N4 to improve its specific surface area and porosity, therefore; mass transport capability and photocatalytic process of fabricated g-C3N4 can be further increased. Thus, this process merely concentrates on morphological improvement of original g-C3N4, and typical problem of rapid charge carriers’ recombination was not being solved. So that, some other techniques concentrating on bandgap optimization or charge separation proficiency of g-C3N4 were proposed like heteroatom doping [361–365], dye sensitizing [366–368] and coupling with new semiconductors [369–371]. Recently, Liu et al. [372] elucidated the engineering of new type of CQD reformed porous g-C3N4 composite (CNC) photocatalyst using a simple polymerization approach. In this scheme, fabricated CQDs have been mixed with urea in calculated ratio, adapted with polymerization and calcination techniques to attain CNCs. Consequently, the photocatalytic approach of CNCs was examined, and 15 times superior degradation kinetics for diclofenac was observed by CNCs as compared to pristine g-C3N4. This trend was ascribed to better charge carriers’ separation and tuned bandgap, with CQDs connected with surface of g-C3N4 shown in Fig. 29b, c. Furthermore, young carbon nanomaterials (e.g. CNTs and GR) can also be joined with g-C3N4 for additional improvement in photocatalytic activity [373]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting active sites in g-C3N4 framework
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, photocatalytic response is held at photocatalysts' active site and is directly related to photocatalytic activities. Hence, discussing parameters that affect active edges is mandatory for photocatalysis improvement. Typical g-C3N4 has some active sites owing to its restrictions [374–376]. However, improving carbon nitride basal engineering is a better technique to enhance active edges of this photocatalyst. Additionally, hybrid engineering might also be increased g-C3N4 active sites. In this region, combined parameters influence g-C3N4 active sites from these two phases (Fig. 30 )

The structural features and chemical configurations of precursors serve a vital role in planning of photocatalysts. Conventionally precursors tend to coagulate in the bulk during calcination process owing to contact between stacking layers [378–383]. This property greatly binds g-C3N4 active sites and by introducing some functional groups may be reduced conjugate structure, and therefore modify the g-C3N4 morphology. Upto now, several advanced techniques have been modified the precursor that proposed to increase g-C3N4 active site [384–387] e.g. 3D-porous g-C3N4 [377]. Fig. 31 displays a distinct hydrothermal-copolymerization method to fabricate nanocage 3D-porous g-C3N4, having large surface areas generate various new active places that act as additional catalytic active spots. The nano-cage-like 3D-porous structure has been engineered by considering conventional dicyandiamide as a starting material to incorporate urea functional groups

The observed sample possesses various naked active edges that provide almost (20.3-fold) increment in photocatalytic reaction compared to traditional g-C3N4. These outcomes found that precursor modification can be exposed to further active edges of g-C3N4. When several functional groups were doped with a precursor, the structure of attained g-C3N4 varies from conventional one and relies on activity of functional groups. After being preserved with nitric acid, active functional groups (-NHx, -C=O/CO, O-H) have been found onto the external surface of modified g-C3N4 as designated in Fig. 32 . From the experimental data, it is revealed that functional groups like (-C=O and -NHx) and N-defects serve as active sites and increase photodegraded hydrogen elimination; while hydroxyl (–OH) groups give negative performance in photocatalysis [345]. Generally, conventional precursors like (dicyandiamide, melamine, and urea) exhibit micron-sized-particle structures, causing in fabrication of bulk g-C3N4. However, precursor morphology strongly influences that of g-C3N4. Supramolecular self-assembly might be utilized to modify precursor’s structure and subsequently manage g-C3N4 morphology [388–391]. Thus, mostly vacancies and structural defects can fulfill the requirement of an increase in surface area. The vacancies could help in migration of charge carriers, while defects promote recombination of electron carriers, which reduces photocatalytic process. Hence, a photocatalytic reaction can be improved by controlling the number of structural defects and vacancies

The major tactics to bloom g-C3N4 active sites with conventional g-C3N4 modification are the improvement in surface area and surface conductivity treatment. Fig. 30b shows increasing in surface area is generally reflected in the conversion of bulk to g-C3N4 nanosheets and QDs g-C3N4. Mostly, surface-active treatment is an overview of energetic functional groups and surface-active management. Up till now, numerous developed techniques, involving acid-intercalated exfoliation [393], ultrasonicated supported exfoliation [394], and thermally oxidation processes were established to diminish bond between layers and breakage of bulk g-C3N4 into nanosheets [391]. For instance, atomically thin polymeric carbon-nitride nanosheets with oxygen-containing and amino groups [395]. This research points out that introduction of oxygen and amino-containing groups and increasing specific areas can enhance separation of charges and offer more active sites for photodegradation [345,396]. This research offers a new horizon to further explore g-C3N4 with improved photocatalytic activity. In g-C3N4 number of active sites determines the surface activity and for surface activation plasma treatment, a technique which utilizes high energy particles to fall on the material thus activates the surface, [345,396] have been employed on several photocatalytic substances and all of which exhibited much better photocatalytic response afterward. Plasma treatment is a favorable technique for non-metal g-C3N4 photocatalyst because of its constant chemical morphology and comparatively plane surface with its bulk structure [397,398]. In different atmospheric environments, plasma treatment is employed for surface-modification of g-C3N4 [399]. This research emphasis on the plasma enhancement in a hydrogen environment offers a nanosheets assembly for (g-C3N4), which exposes more active spaces and also incorporates hydrophilic group (C-N-H) into photocatalyst to capture water molecules efficiently. This technique is highly recommended for the improvement of photocatalytic active sites and substrate adsorption sites

With the prompt advancement of photocatalysts, based on different nanomaterials, few serious problems for real-world claims have occurred, involving strong coacervation of nano-regime substances that enhances difficulty and budget of photocatalyst separation from applied system whereas lowering service durability [400]. In recent, rapid progress of 3D open-cell substances (aerogels and nanosponges) delivered young directions for engineering photocatalytic response for better reusability and service capability of photocatalysts with fixing them on carbonaceous substrate’s surface using 3D open-cell nanostructures. The advantages of 3D open-cell substrate as a photocatalyst are: (1) 3D open-cell networks can offer a more sophisticated surface for photocatalysts loading; (2) high pores connectivity and porosity can assure fast transfer of reactants; (3) 3D monolithic photocatalysts might be regained from system reaction. For instance, Su et al. [401] applied economical melamine foam (MF) as help, and GO covered its surface to offer high reactive sites for functional substances. One type of metal-organic-frameworks (MOFs), such as ZIF-8 has been selected as a model for ZnO nano-cage production. Therefore, a 3D photocatalytic micro-reactor centered on ZnO nano-cage/rGO/carbon sponge (ZRCs) was engineered with a simple dipped-pyrolysis method (Fig. 33a). The observed photocatalytic micro-reactor indicated exceptional adsorption and interesting photodegradation and H2 creation efficiency plus facile recovery (Fig. 33b-f). The rGO and carbonaceous MF systems were not only treated as conducting medium to ease and photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and efficiently increased solar-light absorption capacity of composite, causing significant improvement in photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 33g). To fabricate 3D monolithic photocatalysts, GR-dependent sponges and aerogels are also found as potential candidates. In the recent past, Hu et al. [402] described a simple method to synthesize functional aerogel, that relies on perylene imide (PI) modified g-C3N4 and GO, which after proper blending is subjected to hydrothermal reaction and followed by freeze-drying converts into composite aerogel The aerogel exhibited significant photocatalytic reaction for NO gas decomposition under UV-light irradiation, which was ascribed to potential visible-light adsorption ability, better charge transfer features and high specific surface area of as-prepared aerogel

Generally, biomass mainly comprised of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen, is originated from plants, animal remains, microorganism, daily waste, etc. In recent decades, natural biomass materials have been studied and exploited as sustainable carbon-rich sources to prepare carbon-based nanomaterials due to some attractive advantages, such as environment friendly, abundant availability, unique morphology and structures with large surface, and good mechanical stability [403,404]. And the characteristics and chemical compositions of prepared carbon-based materials are tailored by the nature of used biomass precursors. Concerning environmental remediation, the development of carbon-based materials from biomass has become a hot topic in recent years. The initial biomass precursor plays a key role in thermal carbonization processes, which can determine the total carbon yields and the production rates of aromatic species; the biomass exhibiting high oxygen contents often results in more defects and lower proportion of aromatic carbon. On the other hand, biomass with high levels of cross-linked and aromatic groups leads to the final carbon yields. Therefore, the biomass precursors, possessing high content levels of carbon, highly cross-linked sub-units, and nitrogen species simultaneously, are favorable for production of nitrogen-doped carbon-based materials with superior conductivity [405]

The plant-derived biomass precursors mainly involve hemicellulose, lignin, cellulose, and a few to name, which is illustrated in Fig. 34 . For example, the dried coconut coir has ~ 46.5 wt% lignin, 21.5 wt% cellulose, and 12.4 wt% hemicellulose [403]. And in dried hemp, cellulose can be up 67 wt%, whilst lignin is only 3.3 wt%.29 And the Scots pine branches and stumps show higher hemicellulose fractions (~ 32% and 28% respectively) [406]. Wei and coworkers [407] utilized camellia petals and ammonium persulfate to obtain S/N co-doped carbon sheets with high BET surface area (> 1,122 m2 g-1). Similarly, Chang et al.[408] successfully used paulownia to prepare nanostructured carbon materials in 2015. Further, Zhu et al. [409] reported on the synthesis of different carbon materials via pyrolysis and subsequent activation by H3BO3, ZnCl2, and KOH using dead ginkgo leaves as biomass precursor, as the leaves usually have many ingredients of saccharides and proteins as well as intrinsic nitrogen functional groups. And then bamboo leaves were transformed into CuOx@carbon nanocomposites with a unique hierarchical structure [410]

Using microorganism-derived biomass as a carbon-rich precursor source has recently expanded the possibilities for producing activated carbon. Proteins, fiber, and lipids are found in microorganism-derived precursors. In contrast, dried Lentinula edodes has about 95% carbohydrate (30.2%), protein (17.1%), fiber (39.5%), and fat (39.5%). (1.9 %) [403]. These components also make up over 90% of the dry weight of several species, including Agaricus bisporus (92%), B. aereus (91.5%), Tricholoma portentosum (90.1%), and Lactarius deliciosus (90%). (92.5 %) [403]. Carbohydrates are the most important of these components in terms of carbon yield because they naturally include a large amount of chitin, which may easily interact with other components and ultimately function as the primary carbon source during the thermal carbonization process. Proteins, fibers, and lipids, on the other hand, produce minimal carbon yields because these constituents disintegrate fast during high-temperature carbonization and emit volatile low-molecular-weight molecules [406]. Due to their rapid growth and abundance, fungi might be used to fabricate carbon compounds. Mushrooms are high in nitrogen. Several studies have found that mushrooms contain high quantities of nitrogen, primarily in the range of 3–10%, and that as a result, they can serve as a readily available precursor for the production of N-doped active carbon materials [406,412]. Recently, Yao et al. [413] used Bradyrhizobium japonicum (nitrogen content: 8–10 wt%) as a precursor material to synthesize 3D hierarchical porous carbon with a high BET surface area (1275 m2g-1) and strong electrical conductivity

Marine detritus, arthropods, crustaceans, and mollusks are natural carbon-rich sources that may be used to generate carbon compounds for environmental purposes. This is due to the presence of chitin, which is the fundamental and most important component of the aforementioned components. Some crustacean species, such as the Cancer (crab) and Nephrops (lobster) genera, have extremely high amounts of chitin (more than 50%) in their bodies, as do the Lepas (goose barnacle) and Carcinus (crab) genera, as well as the Cancer (crab) and Nephrops (lobster) genera [414]. A similar pattern was seen in numerous common types of insects, including Diptera pupae (54.8 percent chitin concentration) and Pieris pupae (54.8 percent chitin content) (64.0 percent) [414]. Chitin is one of the most prevalent biopolymers found in nature, second only to cellulose in terms of abundance. When it comes to amino-polysaccharides, chitin stands out because it has a high concentration of critical bio-renewable nitrogen (7 wt.% percent), as well as being extremely chemically and thermally durable [415–417]. Because of this, chitin can produce a larger yield of carbon than cellulose when compared to cellulose. Notably, chitin not only forms crosslinking networks with catecholamine and glucan through bio-crosslinking, but it is also capable of forming strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds with other molecules [418]. This reveals why chitin has far higher chemical/thermal stability than cellulose, giving it a superior alternative for manufacturing carbon compounds. Animal remnants also include keratin, which is an essential component in addition to chitin. A majority of the time, chitin is the primary ingredient of sloughs and cuticles, whereas the majority of keratin is found in the hair of animals, as well as their claws and horns [419]. Keratin, a kind of fibrous structural protein, exhibits exceptional chemical and thermal stability as a result of the formation of strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds between its molecules. Aside from that, strong covalent disulfide cystine crosslinking and non-covalent hydrogen bonds exist at the same time within the molecular structure of keratin (see Fig. 35 ), resulting in significantly improved stability during thermal carbonization processes and, as a result, significantly higher carbon yields [405]. In 2014, Qian et al. [420] reported that they had successfully manufactured heteroatom-doped nanoporous carbon flakes by carbonization, utilizing human hair fibers as the starting precursor to doing this. According to Rangel-Mendez [420], shrimp heads (Litopenaeus sp.) were used as the primary source to extract chitin via lactic acid fermentation, followed by the formation of chitosan, which demonstrated spectacular efficiency (up to 68 %) in the removal of lead(II) from aquatic environment [421]. Recently, shrimp shells have also been used as a chitin-rich starting material for the production of ultrathin chitin nanofibers, which are used to remove arsenic metal ions from water and other liquids. Animal remains, due to their superior qualities, can be used as a primary source for chitin or keratin extraction, opening the door to novel, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly techniques to carbon material synthesis [422]

Mui et al. [425] studied the MB removal over bamboo-derived char with a 327 m2g-1 surface area that had an MB adsorption capacity of 319.2 mg/g, which is attributed to the unique porous structures prompted by pyrolytic char derivation step at 1173 K. At such temperature, the lignocellulose components in bamboo degrade and possess high thermal resilience. While, the char showed far less effectiveness in the adsorption of AB25 and AY117 acidic dyes, which may be hindered by their large molecular sizes. Thus, Xiang et al. [426] modified the synthetic method by ultrasonic-treatment of bamboo fiber with glycine and KHCO3 before the 1073 K carbonization process. Furthermore, Mui et al., [425] halved the duration of thermal treatment to merely 1h (achieved higher MB adsorption capacity (816.0 mg/g) at optimum condition, which may be attributed to the electron-rich N (from 0.36% to 2.49%) and O species (from 13.80% to 34.42%) by glycine to the resultant char, giving rise to larger surface (2037.5 m2g-1) and interface wettability for augmented MB adsorption. Next seed hair fibers of metaplexis japonica also were used to prepare activated carbon fibre (abbreviated as MACFs, surface area of 1882.0 m2g-1) for adsorptive MB removal [427]. Specifically, MACFs gave a high MB adsorption capacity of 943.37 mg/g under optimized conditions. Similarly, Wang et al. [427] adopted a distinctive pre-treatment method: a phosphoric acid-activation of biomass precursor before the 873 K carbonization (for 70 mins). In the acid-activation pre-treatment, the electrophilic P-species can incorporate into the fibers to improve the hydrophilic property of carbonized product (wettability with the contact angle 33.5o vs non-treated sample 83.2o, Fig. 36 ), which can promote the dispersion of MACFs in aqueous solution (inset of Fig. 36), consequently enhanced the adsorptive performance. To improve the adsorptive performance, other functionalities also can be introduced into biomass-derived adsorbent via doping strategy. For instance, Guo et al. [428] developed a magnetically-modified rice husk biochar (MBC) with Fe-doping. An 8.49 wt% of Fe was hydrothermally-amalgamated into MBC at 353 K, synchronously induced a 100% surface area increment (from 52.1 m2 g-1 to 109 m2 g-1), which is attributed to the roughening of surface. Thereby enhanced adsorption of phenanthrene of 41.0 mg g-1 over MBC was obtained (vs 16.2 mg g-1 over unmodified RH biochar) in aqueous phase. Further, the MBC was well applied in the removal of medical waste (e.g. Tetracycline, TC). Chen et al. [429] investigated the adsorptive TC removal over rice husk ash (RHA, constituent of mainly carbon-based lignocellulose) and inorganic species and its performance on initial TC concentration, temperature, and pH. The removal efficiency was plummeted from 60.9% (at initial TC concentration of 5 mg L-1) to 34.4% (20 mg L-1), and the TC uptake by RHA was improved from 1.51 mg/g in 5 mg L-1-system to 3.41 mg/g in 20 mg L-1-system, which is due to the high transportive flux in concentrated systems. Under different pH conditions, TC as cationic form (TCH3+ at pH < 2 and TCH2+ zwitterion at pH of 3.3-7.7) is resistive against the adsorption process over the positively-charged RHA due to the electrostatic repulsion. Under an alkaline environment, the TCH− or TC2− also repelled against negatively-charged RHA. Nonetheless, alkali-induced surface activation could concurrently be prompted to RHA, thereby yielded to its pore opening and hence, the enhanced physicochemical properties for TC adsorption. As results, the detrimental repulsion in alkaline environment can be nullified, reaching out to TC adsorption at pH 13. Monolayer deposition of TC on RHA’s surface was supported by Langmuir adsorption isotherm model

Carbonaceous nanocomposites hold extraordinary specific surface areas, chemical stability, as well as exceptional thermal, electrical, and optical properties; therefore it has been reflected as most potential aspirants to remove biological and chemical toxins from wastewater. The use of these nanocomposites for wastewater management at the lab scale was the subject of previous studies. Future advances and widespread use of these nanomaterials, however, would inevitably pose several problems, necessitating more intensive testing. The present production approaches for carbonaceous nanomaterials are quite difficult and unproductive; even several studies have been directed to challenge it. Additionally, vigorous and competent fabrication techniques are immediately required. Although commercial extensive fabrication of carbonaceous nanomaterials is intriguing, it needs to be discussed because of the wide variety of applications. The effectiveness of carbonaceous nanomaterials in handling physical natural and wastewater from various factories must be confirmed; almost all of the experiments were performed in lab settings. Furthermore, research into the effectiveness and application of various carbonaceous nanomaterials in wastewater handling can be performed under more precise conditions. In addition, the persistent growth of carbonaceous nanomaterials must be scrutinized. In addition, there are still many problems and challenges here, and we need further research and exploration, which is outlined in ongoing headings

More Studies on fullerene and semiconductor photocatalysts are required for practical purposes, as determining their selectivity in multi-component solutions, and chemical stability at various pH and temperature levels. It is still difficult to find suitable qualitative studies on the impact of surface composition on semiconductor nanomaterial interaction. The preparation of fullerene and derivatives is time-consuming and costly. To our understanding, the purification of fullerenes is the most important factor in determining the fullerene price and its applicability. To make the procedure easier, need to improve fullerene purification technologies. Also, fullerene C60 and derivatives are used in minute amounts in photocatalysis. Theoretical modeling of fullerene-semiconductor composite features helps uncover photocatalytic process and optimize composite of fullerene-semiconductor composition and structure to boost photo-quantum efficiency. Our researchers use theoretical calculations to create novel photocatalytic cracking agents, modify the internal characteristics and microstructure of fullerene-based composites, and design the electronic structure of active surfaces. To properly depict the internal response process, future studies should combine experimental and theoretical simulations. The heterojunction configuration helps boost photocatalytic activity. Despite this, research on these topics is currently limited. There is still much room for fullerene-modified composite catalyst design control. The treatment of water contaminants focuses on organic dyes and heavy metal ions. More emphasis needs to be placed on treating persistent organic pollutants like antibiotic contaminants

Currently, bottom-up method has been applied to synthesize controlled SWCNT diameters but failed to get longer SWCNTs even in millimeter range, which is required for developing vertically aligned CNTs membranes. Therefore, novel or tuned method to synthesize controlled and cost-effective SWCNTs is an urgent need to bloom CNT application in water purification fields. Synthesis of homogenous CNTs with perfect geometry for high power density is also appreciable for upgrading this technology. One-pot combination of CNTs with classical electrochemical techniques could be a nice electrical device for water pollution control. It could increase the overall catalysis rate, fast mass transport, good control over the classical current flow in small geometry. Pristine CNTs are often contaminated with various metal catalysts, ash, and carbonaceous agents. These act as additional adsorbent sites of CNTs for multiple water pollutants. Although several approaches such as gas and liquid phase reactions, direct oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, and wet chemical purifications have been adopted to remove CNT impurities and manipulated functionalities, they might degrade CNT, shortening and burning under extreme heat and excessive chemicals. Impurities removal with maintaining original nanotube integrity is one of the toughest tasks in CNT-based water purification applications. As a result of low separability, CNTs in wastewater treatment produce secondary contamination. These water purification methods must modify CNT solubility in water systems in addition to CNT purification. In order to remove pollutants from water, it is necessary to introduce water into the reactive surfaces of pollutants in order to adsorb them. The hydrophobic graphite coating on pristine CNTs prevents water solubility. Different covalent and noncovalent functionalization techniques have been used. To manipulate CNT solubility and imperfections, moist oxidizing agents are used to clean water

In order to resolve the faultiness of CNTs, it is possible to modify them using magnetic metal nanomaterials. It is necessary to improve the stability of carbon nanotube-based materials to prevent metal leaching. Another potential research avenue for improving stability is the construction of CNTs filters. Now, most CNT-based materials research deals exclusively with single organic contaminants. Furthermore, practical wastewater is typically complicated, containing metal ions, anions, humic acid, and organic solvents, among other components. An in-depth study of CNT-based materials' potential in wastewater treatment is required. The ultimate goal of CNT-based materials in wastewater treatment is the industrialization of the treating wastewater. To achieve commercial criteria, practical applications should depend on financial and operational feasibility

The promise of this flourishing sector has yet to be completely realized despite quick and significant advancement. A few specialized applications for water treatment have already shown the potential of GR and derivative-enabled technologies. But how these technologies may be integrated into current systems demands comprehensive review. To be widely used, they must be mass-produced. We urgently need an industrial-scale, ecologically friendly way to make GR. However, the innovation in GR synthesis enables the GR mass production at a reasonable cost by mechanical-shearing exfoliation of graphite in an aqueous system, which was previously impossible. To develop various derivatives and multifunctional hybrid macro-sized nanomaterials using GR as a precursor would require considerable research effort. In order to increase GR's activity, stability, and selectivity for target species, more research is required

As easy to produce as GR membranes are, they nevertheless face challenges. The size, degree of oxidation, chemical bonding, and density of nanopores of GR and GO membranes must be controlled for highest results. Large molecules can pass through the barrier because of the swelling of GO in water. In this regard, better manufacturing methods are required to enhance self-assembled film structure. Large molecules can pass through the barrier because of the swelling of GO in water. In this regard, improved production methods are required to enhance self-assembled film structure. The separation efficacy of GO membranes is affected by cation-GO interactions, small molecules adsorption, and pH-dependent oxidative degradation. These membranes' separation efficiency and structure in complicated feed solutions require additional research. GO membranes should also be able to regulate their performance for various applications. To widely use GO membranes, industrial preparation is required. In the long term, huge films should indeed be developed

GR's fascinating but often overlooked characteristics as semiconductors and co-catalysts should be explored extensively in photocatalysis research. It is important to study GR's semiconductor properties in order to develop highly efficient photocatalysts. They were unique from ideal single-layer GR with zero-bandgap, and their electrical characteristics may lead to surprising photocatalytic improvement. Achieving large-scale and high throughput fabrication of defect-free single-layer pure GR sheets remains a major priority. Because pure GR has no hydrophilic functional groups, making semiconductor/GR composites can be difficult. Defects in rGO sheets must be reduced to restore their conductivity. These techniques should be carefully defined from a system engineering perspective. It is possible to develop and fabricate extremely effective GR-based composite photocatalysts as the next-generation artificial photocatalytic systems using an interface, composite, and structural (morphology control) engineering. The following variables are crucial for boosting the overall photocatalytic performance of GR-based composite photocatalysts, however. We expect that the extensive collaboration of many advantages of GR and semiconductor will constantly lead to unique GR–semiconductor composite materials functions, mechanisms, and applications in many scientific and industrial communities. The study focus is still on oil/water separation, dye removal, membranes, and photocatalysis, for example. Disinfection and elimination of persistent organic contaminants are very unusual applications that require further investigation

There are still numerous difficulties and obstacles to be solved, even though active sites in g-C3N4 photocatalysis have been identified. Complex g-C3N4 based nanocomposites with well-designed designs pose significant problems. Researchers must carefully examine how different semiconductors interact with g-C3N4 to change their electrical and lattice structure. In order to better understand how g-C3N4 based photocatalysts work, as well as to build efficient photoreactor to remove organic/inorganic pollutants, computational or modeling studies are necessary. G-C3N4-based nanocomposites should have increased catalytic activity and stability. Despite great degrading efficiency, mineralization rate can be improved. Some g-C3N4 nanocomposites are also unstable because of active site loss and metal species leaching. Making g-morphology C3N4's more active requires the most effort. Its bulky nature covers several active sites and active edges, reducing its specific surface. Aside from boosting active sites, changing morphologies has other benefits. Defects in g-C3N4 that aren't favorable for photocatalysis might result from raising the specific surface area. As a result, the relationship between active sites and specific surface area might be obscured achieving a balance between specific surface area and structural flaws is difficult. Second, surface activity therapy improves g-C3N4 activation. Enriching photocatalytic active sites with functional groups and surface structures Regular treatment of g-C3N4 can degrade its surface

The addition of active functional groups to precursors is also praiseworthy. Because of their chemical interaction to produce the delocalized-conjugated structure, it is difficult to separate nitrogen and carbon atoms for oxidation and reduction. Conjugated structures are delocalized and active surface sites are increased when heteroatom doping and heterojunction structures are used to modify the energy band structure of g-C3N4. Moreover, many atoms or catalysts have specialized adsorption to only one or a few substrates, restricting their use. To deactivate traditional g-C3N4, future research should suggest moderate, green methods. Studies on the degrading activity of g-C3N4 based nanocomposites are required

The production cost and property tuning must be addressed to provide efficient water treatment results. In addition, clean aerogels are quite difficult to produce. For adsorbent renewal in many water treatment applications, improving aerogel compressive strength might be extremely beneficial. In order to successfully remove contaminants, aerogels need to have many functional groups and a large specific surface area. To eliminate pollutants, materials must have various characteristics. However, despite the potential of aerogels as adsorbents, further research is needed. Remember that producing aerogels often necessitates very energy-intensive methods (i.e., freeze-drying or CO2). The material generated may then be used to determine the optimal reactor setup and drying time. A similar study might enhance experimental conditions and the cost feasibility of aerogel preparation. Recyclability and reusability of aerogels must also be considered. The utilization of waste items as raw materials for aerogel production is another area that requires further exploration. The paper industry, for example, is an important source of cellulose, which is used to make aerogels with good mechanical and adsorption characteristics. To cut expenses and create a new method of recycling some resources, this technique is appropriate. Finally, the aerogels' unique structure allows for surface engineering and functionalization of the aerogels. The development of materials that may eliminate or detect pollution will require more research

Carbon-biomass composites can operate as electronic bridges, enhancing charge carrier lifetimes. Furthermore, there are a number of study gaps that require more investigation. The first point is that high-temperature carbonization is irreplaceable, thus energy consumption is critical. Because of this, scaling up is difficult. Second, no research on the economics of employing biomass-derived carbon-modified semiconductors has been published yet. There are two major issues to consider: financial and ecological. Also, most contemporary research is done in the lab. This biomass could be used as economical carbon-rich precursors for environmental remediation, in addition to plentiful biomass derived from nature. Thus, large-scale biomass carbon production must be improved soon. Also, using different biomass feed stocks and synthesis conditions can significantly alter the properties of the produced carbon compounds. The appropriate beginning biomass content and accompanying synthetic parameters require more investigation. Carbon materials' morphologies, physical-chemical characteristics, and photocatalytic performances will need to be studied in future. For industrial biomass waste, which may contain dangerous compounds like heavy metals, it is necessary to examine environmental concerns. The photocatalytic breakdown of contaminants may be inhibited by the biomass precursor. The conversion of plentiful biomass into carbon compounds can certainly help reduce global waste. So future environmental cleanup advances will be tremendous

The environmental and unique refining of heavy metals from respective wastewater pollutants has been explored using carbon nanostructures. These carbon nanocomposites have been successfully employed to decontaminate wastewater of heavy metals. The motivation for effective use originates from their appealing properties, which include rapid recycling rates, patience required to release the adsorbed metallic ions, and a large surface area. Furthermore, carbon nanomaterials are readily manufactured and functionalized with other nanomaterials, causing a multifunctional nano-adsorbent. Carbon-based fabrics are very much biocompatible for atmosphere and living organisms. Furthermore, various factors such as contact time, pH value, and adsorbent form are essential. On the basis of this literature review, it is feasible to conclude that carbon nanomaterials have fascinating physicochemical features and exhibit a high response when utilized in wastewater treatment and environmental remediation
Ali Raza: Conceptualization, Data curation. Shahnila Altaf: Writing – original draft. Sarfraz Ali: Formal analysis. Muhammad Ikram: Writing – review & editing. Gao Li: Writing – review & editing.
